text:
Good evening, friends, or afternoon, rather. I'm happy to be here this afternoon. And if there is any good thing, let it be for the glory of God.
If Mr. Jackson is in here from South Africa, Brother Jackson, if he is in the meeting this afternoon, Billy wants to see you at the book concession right away, Brother Jackson, about arrangements for tonight on leaving, if you will. He told me to announce that he wanted to meet you at the book stand right now. All right. And Billy, wherever you are, why, Brother Jackson will go to the book stand right away.
Now, to the audience, I wish to address you this afternoon in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ. As God has given me this privilege, along with you, to be His representative, His servant, and His child, by grace, through Jesus Christ. Many of us have many things that we would … could tell. Each one could get up here and tell a life story. Many of us probably would be full of victory and power, and many of us would be full of heartaches and disappointments.
text_no:
God kveld, venner, eller ettermiddag, rettere sagt. Jeg er glad for å være her denne ettermiddagen, og la alt som er godt være til Guds ære.
Hvis Mr. Jackson fra Sør-Afrika er her, Bror Jackson, hvis du er i møtet denne ettermiddagen, så vil Billy treffe deg ved bokstanden med en gang. Bror Jackson, det gjelder ordninger for kvelden, så vennligst møt Billy ved bokstanden nå. Billy ba meg kunngjøre at han ønsker å møte deg ved bokstanden umiddelbart. Greit, og Billy, hvor enn du er, vil Bror Jackson gå til bokstanden straks.
Nå til forsamlingen, ønsker jeg å tale til dere denne ettermiddagen i vår Herre Jesus Kristus' navn. Gud har gitt meg denne privilegiet, sammen med dere, å være Hans representant, Hans tjener og Hans barn, av nåde gjennom Jesus Kristus. Mange av oss har mye vi kunne fortelle. Hver enkelt kunne stå her og fortelle sin livshistorie. Mange livshistorier ville sannsynligvis være fulle av seier og kraft, mens andre ville være fulle av hjertesorg og skuffelser.

text:
And we each one have a life that God has given us, and we must live it. And I … to my humble opinion, if you will get this, I think the most best life in the world, no matter whether it is up or down, if we will find God's path and walk in it, where God has ordained for us to walk. If we always, we find victory no matter … I think of blind Fanny Crosby when she was sitting there in the darkness, the question was once asked, "What think ye of Christ? Whose Son is He?"
And I think of all the men, and great men down through the ages, any man that ever amounted to anything mostly, were men and women who believed Jesus Christ. Isn't that right? And I think of how the prophets wrote of Him and how the ancient men, they foretold of Him, and how the patriarchs, how they … the rulers who raised against Him was brought low, and so forth.
text_no:
Hver av oss har et liv som Gud har gitt oss, og vi må leve det. Etter min ydmyke mening, hvis du vil ta dette til deg, tror jeg at det beste livet i verden, uansett om det går opp eller ned, er å finne Guds vei og vandre i den, der Gud har forordnet at vi skal gå. Om vi alltid gjør dette, vil vi finne seier uansett hva.
Jeg tenker på blinde Fanny Crosby som satt der i mørket, da hun en gang ble spurt: "Hva mener du om Kristus? Hvis Sønn er Han?" Og jeg tenker på alle menn og store mennesker gjennom tidene; enhver som har betydd noe, var stort sett menn og kvinner som trodde på Jesus Kristus. Er ikke det riktig?
Jeg tenker på hvordan profetene skrev om Ham, hvordan de gamle menn forutsa Hans komme, og hvordan patriarkene gjorde det samme. Herskerne som reiste seg mot Ham ble bragt ned, og så videre.

text:
And I think down through the age, I think of the father of our nation, George Washington, how he trusted God. I think of Abraham Lincoln. Lincoln, of course, I don't … not a politician, but Lincoln was my favorite amongst all the presidents we have ever had. He had to come up the hard way, maybe because I had to come that way is the reason I sympathize with Lincoln: splitting rails, and writing in the dirt, and so forth. And the only books that we believe that Lincoln ever had until he was twenty-one years old, was the Bible and the Foxe Book Of Martyrs. That's what molded that character.
Let me see what you read, let me go in your office, in your house, and see what you read, I will--I will tell you what you are. That's right. Everything is with nature. And you keep the Bible laying close for your children, read it yourself, be an example. That's what I didn't have in my younger life. But by God's grace I want to put that before my children. And if there is another generation, may they put it before theirs. And now, if we could think today…
I heard you when I come in last night. My heart was thrilled when you were singing: All Hail The Power Of Jesus' name, let angels prostrate fall.
text_no:
Jeg tenker på historien og reflekterer over personer som George Washington, vår nasjons far, og hvordan han stolte på Gud. Abraham Lincoln er min favoritt blant alle presidentene, selv om jeg ikke er en politiker. Lincoln måtte kjempe seg oppover i livet, kanskje derfor jeg føler sympati for ham, ettersom jeg også har hatt det tøft: kløyve ved og skrive i jorda, og så videre. De eneste bøkene Lincoln hadde frem til han var tjueen år, var Bibelen og "Foxe Book of Martyrs". Det var disse bøkene som formet hans karakter.
La meg se hva du leser, la meg gå inn på kontoret ditt eller hjemmet ditt, og se hva du leser; jeg vil kunne fortelle deg hvem du er. Alt henger sammen med naturen. Hold Bibelen nær for dine barn, les den selv, vær et eksempel. Dette hadde jeg ikke i min ungdom, men ved Guds nåde vil jeg gi dette til mine barn. Og hvis det kommer en ny generasjon, måtte de gjøre det samme for sine.
Da jeg kom inn i går kveld, ble hjertet mitt fylt av glede da jeg hørte dere synge: "All Hail The Power Of Jesus' Name, let angels prostrate fall".

text:
Just late, Dr. DeWitt, when he was dying, he was standing before his congregation, he was trying to represent Jesus Christ, "Isn't He the greatest of all, He was God, He was Immanuel," and how His power should be in the church and would make them quit their selfishness. He was a pastor of a great church. And his congregation even was against him. They were waiting here for conference so they could vote him out, and so forth, and send him away.
But his heart was a bleeding. And so then while he was preaching his heart out one day, he had a heart attack, and fell forward. There happened to a physician in the church, come to him and said, "Dr. DeWitt, you just have a few minutes longer to live. You can't make it."
He called for two faithful deacons who held up his hands. And he got his hands up and steadied to his feet, and said, "Let me stand on my feet, as long as there is breath in my body."
text_no:
Rett før sin død, sto Dr. DeWitt foran sin menighet og forsøkte å representere Jesus Kristus. "Er ikke Han den største av alle? Han var Gud, Han var Immanuel," sa han, og understreket hvordan Hans kraft skulle være i menigheten og få dem til å slutte med egoismen. Han var pastor for en stor menighet, men selv menigheten var imot ham. De ventet på konferansen slik at de kunne stemme ham ut og sende ham bort.
Hans hjerte var knust. Mens han en dag prekte av hele sitt hjerte, fikk han et hjerteinfarkt og falt fremover. Det fantes en lege i menigheten som kom til ham og sa: "Dr. DeWitt, du har bare noen få minutter igjen å leve. Du vil ikke klare det."
Han kalte på to trofaste diakoner som støttet ham. Han fikk hendene opp, stabiliserte seg på føttene og sa: "La meg stå på mine føtter så lenge det er pust i min kropp."

text:
Behind him was the cross that represented the cross--the cross of Christ, back there, by his baptistery. And he stood up like that, he said, "If I have one word I want to say, is this, all hail the power of Jesus' name, let angels prostrate fall. Bring forth the royal diadem, and crown Him Lord of all." He started staggering backwards like that, when he went backwards, he throwed one arm around one side of the cross, and one, the other, and throwed his head down, and went to meet the Lord. Hallelujah. That's the way to go.
I think of Paul Rader, that great gallant hero who stormed Chicago, about the last revival you've ever had in Chicago. When Paul Rader stood there, went out there and he was among his own people had put him to grief, and sorrow, and upset which gave him a cancer. And after while, died. The people that was against him, and doing so was the ones who done it. When he was… Little Moody Bible Institute over here had the little quartet, as I understand, out there singing for him. They had the window shades pulled down, and he was dying. And Paul was quite a cut up. Puts me in mind of Brother Bosworth. He's always has a little sense of humor.
text_no:
Bak ham var korset som representerte Kristi kors, plassert der ved dåpsbassenget. Han reiste seg og sa: "Hvis jeg har ett ord å si, så er det dette: Lovpriset være Jesu navn, la engler falle ned i tilbedelse. Ta frem den kongelige diadem og kron Ham som alles Herre." Han begynte å vakle bakover, og da han gikk bakover, kastet han den ene armen rundt den ene siden av korset og den andre armen rundt den andre siden, bøyde hodet og gikk for å møte Herren. Halleluja. Det er slik man skal gå.
Jeg tenker på Paul Rader, den store helten som stormet Chicago under den siste vekkelsen i byen. Da Paul Rader sto der, var han blant sine egne som hadde påført ham sorg og bekymring, noe som ga ham kreft. Etter en tid døde han. De menneskene som var imot ham og utførte dette mot ham, var de ansvarlige for hans lidelse. På Moody Bible Institute hadde de en liten kvartett som, etter det jeg forstår, sang for ham. De hadde trukket ned vinduene, og han var i ferd med å dø. Paul var kjent for å ha et godt humør, noe som minner meg om Bror Bosworth, som også alltid hadde en liten humoristisk sans.

text:
And so he looked around, he seen the curtains all down, he come to himself, looked around and said, "Say, who's dying here, me or you?" Said, "Raise them shades and sing me some good Gospel songs; snappy." And they got to singing: Down At the Cross Where My Savior Died," or something like that, he said, "that sounds better."
Says, "Where's Luke?" And Luke was back in the other room, they brought Luke in to where he was. He took hold of his hand, and said, "Luke, we've come a long ways together, brother, down through the shady lanes." But said, "Think of it. In five minutes from now, I will be standing in the presence of Jesus Christ, clothed in His righteous." He died.
Lives of great men all remind us
We can make our lives sublime,
With partings, leave behind us
Footprints on the sands of time.
text_no:
Han så seg rundt og la merke til at gardinene var trukket for. Han kom til bevissthet, så seg om og sa: "Si meg, hvem er det som skal dø her, meg eller deg?" Han sa: "Trekk opp gardinene og syng noen gode evangeliesanger for meg; glade." Da begynte de å synge "Ved korset der min Frelser døde" eller noe lignende. Han sa: "Det høres bedre ut."
Han spurte: "Hvor er Lukas?" Lukas var i et annet rom, så de hentet ham. Han tok Lukas i hånden og sa: "Lukas, vi har gått langt sammen, bror, ned gjennom skyggelagte veier. Men tenk på det, om fem minutter vil jeg stå i Jesu Kristi nærvær, kledd i Hans rettferdighet." Så døde han.
Store menns liv minner oss om
At vi kan gjøre våre liv edle,
Og etterlatte spor i tiden.

text:
Part of his brothers travel… Think of Lincoln, when he was shot there because of his gallantry and standing for humane and what was right for God. Told when he was going to die, when they … the bullet that went through his … below his … in his body there, and he was smothering to death, he said, "Turn my head towards the setting of the sun." He said, "Our Father, Who art in Heaven, hallowed be Thy name. Thy Kingdom come, Thine will be done," repeating the model prayer as he went out to meet God. Oh my! What are we? Men and women…
Look at Eddie Perronett there. He was a persecuted and everything, and what he thought. He wrote the… One day there, when the inspiration hit him, he picked up the pen and wrote the inauguration song: All Hail The Power Of Jesus' name.
I think of [unclear words] there, when he wrote the "Amazing Grace, how sweet the sound that saved a wretch like me." I think of blind Fanny Crosby. "What could God promise you, you never seen daylight in your life? You were blind all your life. What do you think about Jesus Christ?
She said, "Pass me not, O gentle Saviour, hear my humble cry. While on others Thou art calling, do not pass me by. Thou the stream of all my comfort, more than life to me, whom have I on earth beside Thee, or whom in Heaven but Thee?"
Let us, be up and doing,
With a heart for any strife;
Be not like dumb, driven cattle!
Be a hero.
text_no:
En del av hans brødre reiser... Tenk på Lincoln, da han ble skutt på grunn av sin tapperhet og for å stå opp for humanitet og det som var rett for Gud. Han ble fortalt når han skulle dø; da kulen gikk gjennom ham, under hans... i kroppen der, og han kveltes til døde, sa han: "Snu hodet mitt mot solnedgangen." Han sa: "Vår Far, Du som er i himmelen. Helliget vorde Ditt navn. Komme Ditt rike. Skje Din vilje," og gjentok denne bønn mens han gikk for å møte Gud. Å, for en mann! Hva er vi? Menn og kvinner...
Se på Eddie Perronett. Han ble forfulgt og alt, og hva han tenkte. En dag, da inspirasjonen slo til, tok han opp pennen og skrev innvielsessangen: "All Hail The Power Of Jesus' Name."
Jeg tenker på John Newton der, da han skrev "Amazing Grace, how sweet the sound that saved a wretch like me." Jeg tenker på den blinde Fanny Crosby. "Hva kunne Gud love deg, som aldri har sett dagslys i ditt liv? Du var blind hele ditt liv. Hva tenker du om Jesus Kristus?"
Hun sa: "Pass meg ikke, o milde Frelser, hør mitt ydmyke rop. Mens Du kaller på andre, ikke gå forbi meg. Du, min trøstes kilde, mer enn livet for meg; hvem har jeg på jorden ved siden av Deg, eller hvem i himmelen uten Deg?"
La oss stå opp og handle,
Med et hjerte for enhver kamp;
Ikke vær som stumme, drevne kveg!
Vær en helt.

text:
Each one of you is a Christian. You are a born-again Christian, then let's stand up. No matter how bad the background has been, let's look forward now to the Coming of our Lord, when this mortal will take on immortality. Back to…
A few moments now. Try not to keep you no longer. Already, I'm past time; twenty minutes after three. I'm … Go about an hour. I will try to be finished if I can. I … Many of you here has probably has heard the life story, things that I hate to go back over, but I…
One of my greatest altar calls I ever made in America, I had two thousand sinners to come to Jesus Christ in Pensacola, Florida after the life story one afternoon. I trust to God, that was next to the Durban where we had thirty thousand.
Now I want to read a portion of Scripture, always God's Word, because my word fails, but God's Word can't fail. Now, found in the 13th chapter of Hebrews, beginning with the 10th verse, and reading the 14th verse inclusive:
We have an altar, whereof they who have no right … which serve … tables.
For the bodies of those beasts, whose blood is brought into the sanctuary by the high priest for sin, are burnt without the camp.
Wherefore Jesus also, that he might sanctify the people with his own blood, suffered without the gate.
Let us go forth therefore unto him without the camp, bearing his reproach.
For here we have no continuing city, but we seek one to come.
text_no:
Hver enkelt av dere er en kristen. Dere er gjenfødte kristne, la oss da stå opp. Uansett hvor vanskelig fortiden har vært, la oss nå se frem mot Herrens gjenkomst, når det dødelige skal ikle seg udødelighet.
Et øyeblikk nå. Jeg skal prøve å ikke holde dere lenger. Jeg er allerede på overtid; klokken er tjue minutter over tre. Jeg går omtrent en time, og jeg skal prøve å avslutte innen det. Mange av dere her har sannsynligvis hørt livshistorien min, ting som jeg misliker å repetere, men jeg...
En av mine største alterkall i Amerika hadde jeg i Pensacola, Florida, hvor to tusen syndere kom til Jesus Kristus etter livshistorien min en ettermiddag. Jeg stoler på Gud, at dette var like nært som i Durban hvor vi hadde tretti tusen.
Nå vil jeg lese en del av Skriften, alltid Guds Ord, fordi mine ord kan feile, men Guds Ord kan ikke feile. Funnet i Hebreerne 13, begynner med det 10. verset og leser til og med det 14. verset:
Vi har et alter, som de som tjener ved tabernaklet ikke har rett til å spise av.
For blodet av de dyr som ble brakt inn i helligdommen av ypperstepresten som et sonoffer, ble brent utenfor leiren.
Derfor led også Jesus utenfor byporten for å hellige folket med Sitt eget blod.
La oss derfor gå ut til Ham utenfor leiren, og bære Hans vanære.
For her har vi ikke en varig by, men vi søker den som skal komme.

text:
How many of you is away from home today, let's see your hands; away from home? My, just looky here. As I think, if I had time we would sing that song: we're pilgrims and we're strangers here, were seeking a city to come, is that right? No matter where you ever roam, there will be no place who … that will ever take homes place. Is that right?
Wouldn't you just like to take a little trip today? Most all of you here are my age, or maybe a little above. And wouldn't you just like to go back to childhood, just spin a little wheel and go back and live another day in childhood? Wouldn't you love to do that? Oh, how I would like to. Even though, with it's sorrows, and tears, and disappointments, I'd like to live one more day of it, just to go back.
text_no:
Hvor mange av dere er borte fra hjemmet i dag? La meg se hendene deres. Å, se her. Jeg tenker, hvis vi hadde tid, kunne vi synget den sangen: Vi er pilegrimer og fremmede her, vi søker en by som skal komme. Er det ikke sant? Uansett hvor du vandrer, vil det aldri være et sted som kan ta hjemmets plass. Er det ikke riktig?
Ville du ikke bare likt å ta en liten reise i dag? De fleste av dere her er på min alder, eller kanskje litt eldre. Ville dere ikke bare likt å gå tilbake til barndommen, snurre på et hjul og leve en dag til som barn? Ville dere ikke elsket det? Å, hvor gjerne jeg ville gjort det. Selv med alle sorger, tårer og skuffelser, ville jeg gjerne levd én dag til av barndommen, bare for å gå tilbake.

text:
I remember the little old place where I come from, and no matter how humble it was … Everyone of you here can remember the old place where mother used to stand under the tree, perhaps, on an old cedar wash tub with a wash board, you was a little girl or boy, playing around. Many time … you remember that, the many heartaches, and sorrows that went through, how you pulled unto her an old spotted apron. like to see her again today, but that can't be now. No, she's gone on.
Like to see old Dad, when I used to see him come from the field with that red handkerchief sticking in his pocket. See him get up of a morning, on a cold morning, go back and make a fire in a big old drum stove. I used to hear him sing:
Oh, where is my boy tonight,
My heart overflows
For a loving he knows,
Oh, where is my boy tonight?
I seen him stand by the little old wash bench with his sleeves rolled up, and washing his face and hands; and he had real black wavy hair. He would look around. Oh, how I would like to see him once more. But I can't, he's gone on. Here we have no continuing city, but we seek one to come. If you could go back to the home where you was raised at, it wouldn't be the home that you was one time.
text_no:
Jeg husker det lille, gamle stedet jeg kommer fra, og uansett hvor beskjedent det var … Alle her kan huske det gamle stedet hvor mor pleide å stå under treet, kanskje ved en gammel vaskebøtte av sedertre med en vaskebrett. Du var en liten jente eller gutt som lekte rundt. Mange ganger … husker du det, de mange hjertesorgene og sorger som gikk forbi, hvordan du dro deg til hennes gamle flekkete forkle. Du ønsker å se henne igjen i dag, men det kan ikke være nå. Nei, hun har gått bort.
Jeg skulle ønske jeg kunne se gamle far, når jeg pleide å se ham komme fra åkeren med det røde lommetørkleet stikkende ut av lommen. Se ham stå opp tidlig om morgenen, på en kald morgen, og lage ild i den store, gamle trommelovnen. Jeg pleide å høre ham synge:
Å, hvor er min gutt i kveld,
Mitt hjerte flyter over
For en kjærlig han vet,
Å, hvor er min gutt i kveld?
Jeg så ham stå ved den lille, gamle vaskeplassen med ermene rullet opp, vaske ansiktet og hendene; han hadde ekte svart, bølgete hår. Han ville se seg rundt. Å, hvor jeg skulle ønske å se ham en gang til. Men jeg kan ikke, han har gått bort. Her har vi ingen blivende by, men vi søker en som skal komme. Hvis du kunne dra tilbake til hjemmet hvor du vokste opp, ville det ikke være det hjemmet det en gang var.

text:
Here, a few days ago, I was taking someone who came to visit me up to where the old home place was. Why, there is a housing project. Well, it isn't the old home anymore. We have no continuing city.
I remember when I … First little old home we lived in was a log house. There was about three or four of us little Branhams out there. We didn't even have a floor, just the dirt. Papa, in the.. right in the middle of the floor, he had a stump that had been sawed off and laid in there, some rock laid on top of it; and old drum stove sitting there. And how that the table, what it was made out of: an old bench that he got some boards off of a barn down there, and sawed a bench out like a church pew like, and set it behind the table.
text_no:
For noen dager siden tok jeg med en besøkende opp til stedet hvor det gamle hjemmet vårt en gang sto. Nå er det et boligprosjekt der. Det er ikke lenger det gamle hjemmet; vi har ingen varig by.
Jeg husker det gamle hjemmet vi bodde i, som var en tømmerhytte. Det var oss tre eller fire små Branhams. Vi hadde ikke engang et gulv, kun jord. Midt på gulvet hadde pappa plassert en stubbe som var saget av, med noen steiner oppå, og på toppen av det sto en gammel trommelovn. Bordet var laget av en gammel benk som han hadde hentet noen planker fra en låve, saget dem til som en kirkebenk, og satt bak bordet.

text:
And Mama had a little old, what we call a monkey stove. Anybody know what a monkey stove is, let's see your… Oh, my, that's fine. And old-fashioned coil lamp. Did you ever clean a lamp chimney? Let's see… Well ju… I'm not the only country boy here. I'm going to take off my coat, and feel right at home. That's right. Yes, sir.
How many of you ever slept on a straw tick? Let's see your hand. Well, say, Chicago is not a big place after all, is it? That's right. My, my, how many times have I slept on an old straw tick. And first time you put in there, maybe feel the grasshopper kicking, have to get up and find him you know, when he was down in there. Why, many times I've done that. Sure.
text_no:
Og mor hadde en liten, gammel "apeovn". Er det noen som vet hva en apeovn er? La meg se… Å, det var bra. Og en gammeldags parafinlampe. Har noen av dere noen gang renset en lampepipe? La meg se… Vel, det ser ut til at jeg ikke er den eneste bygutten her. Jeg tar av meg jakken og føler meg som hjemme. Det er riktig, ja.
Hvor mange av dere har sovet på en halmmadrass? Rekk opp hånden. Vel, ser man det, Chicago er ikke så stor tross alt, eller hva? Det stemmer. Å, hvor mange ganger har jeg sovet på en gammeldags halmmadrass. Første gang du legger deg, kan du kanskje kjenne en gresshoppe sparke, og du må stå opp og finne den, vet du, der den ligger nede i halmen. Ja, mange ganger har jeg gjort det. Helt sikkert.

text:
Seen Mama take that big old stick she had hanging on the wall, a piece of an old… Well, she done … used to … she would use to poke her clothes with out in the--in the yard when she was boiling her clothes. Did you ever boil your clothes? Thank God. Oh, my. Lye soap, you know? And she would use that to put your clothes in. She had a string in it, she would hang it up on the wall.
Now that was herb on that side, but the other side was the--was the golden rule that hung on the other, right over the door, you see. It was a hickory about that long with all the ten commandments wrote out on the end of it. Little boys must behave, and dad believed in the golden rule in that way. So then, if that ever come up missing, there was a razor strap laying back in the back there. That took its place. I tell you, my education was pretty stiff. Dad, I inherited that Irish eyes; flashed like Stonewall Jackson, I knowed something was in for me when I done wrong. But I love him today with all my heart. He never give me half the whippings that I deserve.
And then, I remember mama used to take that stick and smooth out the bed, you know, mash it down, you know, and smooth it out. How many knows what a bolster is? It's a big… Well, what do you know. Say, is anybody here from Kentucky, raise up your hand. Well, my, my, that's really something, isn't it? All right.
text_no:
Jeg husker hvordan mor tok den store stokken hun hadde hengende på veggen, et stykke gammelt... Vel, hun brukte den til å røre klærne sine med ute i gården når hun kokte dem. Har du noen gang kokt klærne dine? Takk Gud. Å, kjære tid. Lut såpe, vet du. Hun brukte den stokken til å løfte klærne med en snor for å henge dem opp på veggen.
På den ene siden hang urter, men på den andre siden, rett over døren, hang den gylne regelen. Det var en hickory-stokk på omtrent den lengden, med alle de ti budene skrevet ut på enden. "Små gutter må oppføre seg," og far trodde på den gylne regelen på den måten. Hvis stokken noen gang manglet, var det en barberstropp liggende bak der som tok dens plass. Min utdannelse var ganske tøff. Far, jeg arvet de irske øynene hans; de glitret som Stonewall Jackson. Jeg visste at noe sto på for meg når jeg hadde gjort noe galt. Men i dag elsker jeg ham med hele mitt hjerte. Han ga meg aldri halvparten av de reprimandene jeg fortjente.
Jeg husker også hvordan mor brukte stokken til å glatte ut sengen, tråkke den ned, og glatte den ut. Hvor mange vet hva en bolster er? Det er en stor... Vel, hvem skulle tro det. Er det noen her fra Kentucky, løft hånden. Vel, kjære tid, det er virkelig noe, ikke sant?

text:
Down in Indiana, or this is Indiana. Down in Southern Indiana, it's some, I laughed there one day in my church, I said, "How many here is from Kentucky," and about two-thirds of them stood up. Some one said … I said, "I don't get it."
And of them said, said, "Brother Branham," said, "ground hogs in Kentucky just took the country." So, coming across from over the other side.
But there, in the front of this little old log cabin, I remember, I used to look at them old chinks, mud in the cracks like that, and I would say, "My, that house will stand forever. Why, it can't go down, what a wonderful place it is." But my, you should see it now, see. Here we have no continuing city.
And around in front of the door was a place wore off, it was just bare slick where we, little bunch of Branham's played out there like a bunch of little opossums, or something, around there. Little bitty fellows, wallering around over one another. Say, I'd like to live that over again. I really would, I say.
text_no:
I Indiana, eller dette er Indiana. I Sør-Indiana, en dag lo jeg i min menighet og sa: "Hvor mange her er fra Kentucky?" Omtrent to tredjedeler reiste seg. Jeg sa, "Jeg forstår det ikke." 
En av dem sa, "Bror Branham, jordsvin i Kentucky tok bare landet." Så de kom fra den andre siden. 
Foran denne lille, gamle tømmerhytta husker jeg at jeg så på de gamle sprekkene, fylt med leire, og tenkte: "Dette huset vil stå for alltid. Hvorfor, det kan ikke falle, for et fantastisk sted det er." Men, du skulle se det nå. Vi har ingen by som består.
Foran døren var et område så slitt at det bare var bart og glatt, der vi, en liten flokk av Branham-unger, lekte som små opossum, eller noe sånt. Små krabater som tumlet rundt sammen. Jeg ville gjerne oppleve det igjen. Det ville jeg virkelig.

text:
I remember the old spring where I used to go down there and lay down on my stomach and drink and drink. Come back up, go out and take dad a jug of water out of the spring back out to the field where he was in harvest or something; worked so hard till I seen my Mama cut his shirt lose from his back from sunburn, where it would stick to his back; seventy-five cents a day to take care of me.
Look, it's true. You have read my life story, out there. My dad did drink, but I don't care what he done, he is still my daddy. And let me tell you something young folks, this afternoon. Don't you never get little enough to call your mother and dad "the old man and woman." You don't never do that, no matter what they are. No matter what they are, you respect them as your dad and mother. You'll never know what, how you love them, till you hear the cat … squeaking of a casket going out, and knowing that's the last of it. It won't be the "old man and woman" then.
text_no:
Jeg husker den gamle kilden hvor jeg pleide å legge meg ned på magen og drikke og drikke. Så kom jeg tilbake, tok en mugge med vann fra kilden og gikk ut til far i åkeren, hvor han jobbet hardt. Jeg har sett mor kutte løs skjorten hans fra ryggen hans på grunn av solbrenthet, der det festet seg til huden. Han jobbet for syttifem øre om dagen for å ta vare på meg.
Hør, det er sant. Dere har lest livshistorien min. Far drakk, men uansett hva han gjorde, er han fortsatt min far. La meg fortelle dere noe, ungdommer: Aldri kalle moren deres og faren deres "den gamle mannen og kvinnen." Uansett hva de er, respekter dem som deres far og mor. Dere vil aldri forstå hvor mye dere elsker dem før dere hører lyden av en kiste som bæres ut, og vet at det er det siste. Da vil det ikke være "den gamle mannen og kvinnen" lenger.

text:
A lot of times they are right, when you think they are wrong. Always, "Honor thy father and mother, which may lengthen the days upon the earth, the Lord thy God giveth thee." That's the first commandment with promise. Be kind to your mother and dad.
I remember my daddy died. He was just beginning to gray a little bit at the temple. When he laid there in the casket and I picked up his head, which had died right on my arm … And I picked up his head and his locks of hair fall down, I thought, "Oh, dad." I looked at his hand. He had had his finger cut off there in the shredder one day. I thought of all the heartaches that I had caused him. It wasn't "the old man," that was my daddy. I don't care who else, what they thought about him, he was still my dad. And I loved him. And I love him today. I had the privilege of leading him to Christ. Now…
And my mother, also. My mother is a living. She is supposed to arrive here this afternoon. And I trust that she will, get here.
text_no:
Ofte har de rett når du tror de tar feil. Husk alltid: "Hedre din far og din mor, så dine dager må bli mange i det landet Herren din Gud gir deg." Det er det første budet med et løfte. Vær snill mot moren og faren din.
Jeg husker da min far døde. Han hadde så vidt begynt å gråne ved tinningen. Da han lå der i kisten, løftet jeg hodet hans, som hadde dødd rett på armen min. Idet jeg løftet hodet hans og hårlokkene hans falt ned, tenkte jeg, "Å, far." Jeg så på hånden hans og husket hvordan han mistet en finger i en kuttemaskin en dag. Jeg tenkte på alle hjertesorgene jeg hadde forårsaket ham. Han var ikke bare "den gamle mannen," han var min far. Uansett hva andre tenkte om ham, var han fortsatt min far, og jeg elsket ham. Jeg elsker ham fremdeles. Jeg hadde privilegiet å lede ham til Kristus.
Min mor lever også. Hun skal komme hit i ettermiddag, og jeg håper virkelig hun gjør det.

text: Now, back in those days, I remember some of the little things, just for details. I remember one thing that out-stood in them days, was, every Saturday night go to town to get the groceries. Did you ever have to do that, go in on a Saturday night and get the food for the week? We lived in the country, and I would work hard all week. I got a dime, when I was a great big boy, twelve, fourteen years old. I got ten cents. Dad said, "Don't spent it all in one place; ten cents." Billy says, "Daddy, you got five dollars you can let me have?" How things have changed. Certainly have.
text_no: Jeg husker noen små detaljer fra de dagene. En ting som virkelig utmerket seg, var at vi dro til byen hver lørdag kveld for å handle dagligvarer. Har du noen gang måttet gjøre det, dra inn på en lørdag kveld for å kjøpe mat for uken? Vi bodde på landet, og jeg jobbet hardt hele uken. Som stor gutt på tolv-fjorten år, fikk jeg en tier. Far sa, "Ikke bruk alt på ett sted; ti øre." Billy sier, "Pappa, har du femti kroner jeg kan få?" Noen ting har virkelig forandret seg. Absolutt.

text:
I remember that ten cents, I would go to town, and, my, go into this store. And I would get my dime's change, and I would get a penny's worth of Redhots, about that many in a sack. They wouldn't even let you look at them, hardly, for a penny now. Then I would go over to get a penny ice cream cone, little bitty old ice cream cone. You get it for a penny. What a day that was. But now it's different.
Then, when we were little bitty lads, I remember when we was all at home, you know, playing around the house, I used to see dad come home, and on Saturday evening, we would all, afternoon, he would get a little old buckboard of affair, jersey wagon; we had a little old mule we hooked to that wagon. And if it was winter time, we would put straw in the back of the--of the wagon, little covered over wagon. And we would get blankets and wrap up.
text_no:
Jeg husker den tiøringen; jeg pleide å dra til byen og gå inn i en butikk. For min tiøring fikk jeg vekslepenger, og jeg kjøpte en pennys verdi av Redhots, som de fylte en liten pose med. Nå ville de knapt la deg se på dem for en penny. Deretter gikk jeg for å kjøpe en liten iskjeks for en penny. For en dag det var. Men nå er det annerledes.
Da vi var små gutter, husker jeg at hele familien var hjemme, og vi lekte rundt huset. Jeg så far komme hjem. På lørdag ettermiddag tok han fram en liten vogn, som var en enkel jerseyvogn. Vi hadde en liten muldyr vi spente for vognen. Om vinteren la vi strå i bakdelen av vognen, som var dekket til, og vi tok tepper og pakket oss inn.

text:
And dad and mother set in the front seat. And down the road they would go, and mother and dad talking you know, they was about twenty-five years old a piece, I guess. And they was sitting up there talking, you know, driving this little old mule. Why, we was in first class. Wasn't our mule or wagon, but we were going somewhere, to the store.
Dad would get about three dollars and a half for the week and he would go down there to spent every bit of it nearly for groceries to feed all those kiddies through the week. We didn't have fried chicken and so forth, but we had to get things that really stuck to the ribs: potatoes, and things like that, that really hung on, went a long ways.
text_no:
Mor og far satt i forsetet, og nedover veien bar det. Mor og far snakket sammen, du vet, de var om lag tjuefem år gamle hver. De satt der og pratet mens de kjørte denne lille gamle mulen. Vi følte oss som førsteklasses passasjerer, selv om det ikke var vår mule eller vogn, men vi var på vei til butikken.
Far ville tjene omtrent tre og en halv dollar i uken og brukte nesten alt på dagligvarer for å mate alle barna gjennom uken. Vi spiste ikke stekt kylling og slikt, men vi måtte skaffe mat som virkelig mettet: poteter og andre ting som varte lenge.

text:
And so I remember when papa pay his grocery bill on Saturday night, that was a treat for the little Branham's. He would get a sack full of candy, little old peppermint stick candy. Say, you know, that was good. I remember when he would come out there, maybe he would have… Maybe there would be four good-sized sticks, and there was five Branhams to divide it between, everyone looking to see if he got his share. Them sticks had to be broken up and divided just exactly the equal among them, because all eyes was turned on that candy.
I guess I cheated little bit on it. All the kiddies would get all they could eat, you know, and they would just eat, and all… They couldn't eat their candy up. I licked mine a little while, you know, and I would reach over and get a piece if that old brown paper sack the meal was wrapped in, and pulled off a little piece if it, and rolled it up, put it in my pocket; I wait till Monday. And so then, I… Monday come along, and Mom would say, "Billy?"
I would say, "Yes ma'am."
text_no:
Jeg husker da pappa betalte dagligvarehandelen på lørdag kveld; det var en glede for de små Branham-barna. Han ville kjøpe en pose full av godteri, små gamle peppermyntestenger. Du vet, det var godt. Jeg husker når han kom ut, og det kanskje var fire store stenger, og vi var fem Branhams som måtte dele dem. Alle sørget for å få sin del. Disse stengene måtte deles nøyaktig likt, for alle øynene var rettet mot godteriet.
Jeg tror jeg jukset litt på det. Alle barna spiste så mye de kunne av godteriet. De spiste til de ikke orket mer. Jeg slikket litt på min, og så tok jeg et stykke av den gamle brune papirposen som maten var pakket inn i, rev av en liten bit, rullet det opp, og la det i lommen. Jeg ventet til mandag. Når mandagen kom, sa mamma: "Billy?"
Jeg svarte: "Ja, mamma."

text:
"Take the bucket." It wasn't one of these little old galvanized buckets, it was big cedar buckets, and an old gourd dipper. How many ever seen a gourd dipper? Oh, that's right. All right, and go down in the spring and pull up the water, you know, and put into the bucket. My, that was a job.
I look over to my brother, I would say, "Tell you what I will do. If you will go get that bucket of water, I will take … I've got my candy yet, I will let you lick it till I count ten slow: one, two, like that." I was a business man. Sat back in the shade, you know, while my brother went and got the water, licked on the candy. Oh, my, I tried to make that ten just about as good as I could, you know. And you ought to seen him lick. My, my. He got more than ten licks off of it all right.
Well, Monday would be a pretty day for me because I keep that piece of candy, you know, just work right on that candy. And they knowed I had it too, you know, so I… Oh, my.
text_no:
"Ta bøtta." Dette var ikke en av disse små, gamle galvaniserte bøttene; det var store bøtter av sedertre, og en gammel kalebassøse. Hvor mange her har sett en kalebassøse? Å, det er riktig. Greit, ned til kilden for å hente vann og fylle bøtta. Å, for en jobb det var.
Jeg ser bort på min bror og sier: "Hør her, hvis du henter den bøtta med vann, vil jeg la deg slikke på godteriet mitt til jeg teller sakte til ti: en, to, på den måten." Jeg var en forretningsmann. Satt i skyggen mens Bror hentet vannet og slikket på godteriet. Å, du verden, jeg prøvde å gjøre den tellingen så lang som mulig. Og du skulle sett ham slikke. Å, min. Han fikk definitivt mer enn ti slikk på det.
Mandag ville være en god dag for meg, for jeg beholdt det stykket godteri og arbeidet med det. Og de visste at jeg hadde det også, vet du, så jeg... Å, min.

text:
I guess today, I could go out and, not on Sunday, but some other day, and buy a box of Hershey's, but it would never taste like that candy. How many of you eat peppermint and old-fashioned barrel crackers, let's see you hands? Oh, my, say, I'm telling you, that wouldn't go bad right now. That's right.
And oh, for meals we would have mulligan stew, was Irish to the core, you know. And my, how many knows what mulligan stew is? Say, that's when you boil everything in the kitchen even to the dish rag almost. Just put everything in a pot and boil it. That's right, get everything in there and boil it up; the turnips, see, the carrots, and the potatoes, and the beans, and the meal. And just put it all together and boil it. Well, almost… That mulligan stew would have to last two or three days, we had it on Sunday. Had beef in it, you know, so it had to be good; quarters worth of beef, my, chunk that big. So then it… Mama would dice it up.
text_no:
I dag kan jeg gå ut og kjøpe en eske med Hershey's, men det vil aldri smake som den godterien. Hvor mange av dere spiser peppermynteknapper og gammeldagse tønnekarameller? La meg se hendene deres. Å, jeg sier dere, det ville ikke vært ille akkurat nå. Det er riktig.
Og når det gjaldt måltider, hadde vi ofte mulligan-stuing, som er irsk til kjernen. Hvor mange vet hva mulligan-stuing er? Det er når du koker alt du har i kjøkkenet, nesten til og med oppvaskkluten. Bare putt alt i en gryte og kok det. Det stemmer, få alt oppi og kok det sammen; kålroten, gulrøttene, potetene, bønnene og maismelet. Bare kombiner alt og kok det. Den mulligan-stuingen måtte vare i to eller tre dager, vanligvis på søndager. Den inneholdt biff, så den måtte være god; en kvart dollars biff, et stort stykke. Så deretter... Moren min hakket den opp.

text: Puts me to mind of Buddy Robinson, when he said he… One time Uncle Buddy said, "I tell you," said, "I went out West and they was having a depression out there." And said, "A great drought was on, had nothing to eat." Said, "The only thing we had was dry apples." Said, "I eat them for breakfast, drink them in water for dinner, swell them up in time for supper." So that's about the way that mulligan stew lasted, just constantly all … till about Wednesday it was played out, had to … then we went back to something else. Great tale.
text_no: Dette minner meg om Buddy Robinson. En gang sa Onkel Buddy: "Jeg dro ut vestover under en økonomisk depresjon." Han sa videre: "Det var en stor tørke, og vi hadde ingenting å spise." Han fortalte: "Det eneste vi hadde var tørkede epler. Jeg spiste dem til frokost, drakk dem i vann til middag og fikk dem til å svelle opp i tide til kveldsmat." Sånn var det med den mulligansuppe – det holdt gående helt til onsdag, da gikk det tomt, og vi måtte finne noe annet. En flott historie.

text:
My, I remember how that back in those days, going to school, I remember brother and I, the one next to me, he's in Glory, too, and how we went to school together. And we would go to school, and we were about the poorest kids there was. We would come across the river from Kentucky, and the Indiana people are just a little more wealthy than that are down in the mountain part of Kentucky, anyhow, where I was borned at. And I, being the only Kentuckian among them, I sure had a hard way to go, I mean I did. they just teased me all the time about being a corn cracker.
And so, I talk real funny, you know. I… It even … I didn't talk plain, maybe not yet, but I … little better job out of it. So I was kind of tongue-tied like, you know, and I would talk funny, and they would laugh at me. And oh, I had an awful time. And ragged? Oh, my!
text_no:
Jeg husker godt hvordan det var da vi gikk på skolen, min bror og jeg. Broren ved siden av meg, som også nå er i Himmelen, og jeg gikk på skole sammen. Vi var de fattigste barna der. Vi kom over elven fra Kentucky, og folkene i Indiana var litt rikere enn de i fjellområdene i Kentucky, hvor jeg var født. Siden jeg var den eneste fra Kentucky der, hadde jeg det virkelig vanskelig. De ertet meg hele tiden og kalte meg "corn cracker."
Jeg snakket også veldig rart. Jeg snakket ikke tydelig, kanskje ikke ennå, men jeg har blitt litt bedre. Jeg var tilbøyelig til å snakke snøvlete, og de lo av meg. Jeg hadde det utrolig tøft. Og slitt? Å, ja!

text:
And I remember, there was one thing about my dad, he would … Now, if he owed a grocery bill, he would go pay that bill. But if he had ten cents left over, he would drink it. Everything he had, he drank up. And that's the reason today I'm so firmly against drinking. The reason I'm so firmly against that stuff is because I knew it ruined my home, and spoiled me from a love that … I always wanted to be loved, if somebody to love me. And even my people in that [unclear words]. Well, I was … I it just didn't have it. And we went to school half-naked. And what a horrible life we had all because of drink. My daddy was a--was a real man, if he just hadn't to have that habit of drinking.
And I know that it is one of the curses of the nation, and I'm against the thing. You say, "Will a little beer hurt you?" You just get born-again and go ahead and drink all the beer you want to, after you get born-again. That's right. You can just drink all you want after you get born-again. But you get born-again first, and that's all you have to do.
text_no:
Jeg husker spesielt én ting om faren min: Hvis han skyldte penger på en dagligvarebutikk, ville han alltid betale regningen. Men hvis han hadde ti øre til overs, ville han bruke det på å drikke. Alt han hadde, drakk han opp. Det er grunnen til at jeg i dag er så sterkt imot alkohol. Jeg er imot alt slikt fordi jeg vet at det ødela hjemmet mitt og frarøvet meg den kjærligheten jeg alltid lengtet etter. Jeg ønsket alltid å bli elsket, av noen, av mine nærmeste. Men det hadde jeg ikke. Vi gikk på skolen delvis nakne. For et forferdelig liv vi hadde, alt på grunn av alkohol. Min far var en god mann, hvis han bare ikke hadde hatt den vanen med å drikke.
Jeg vet at det er en av nasjonens forbannelser, og jeg er imot det. Du spør kanskje, "Vil litt øl skade deg?" Bli født-på-nytt først, og så kan du drikke all den ølen du vil etterpå. Det stemmer. Du kan drikke all øl du ønsker etter å ha blitt født-på-nytt. Men først må du bli født-på-nytt, og det er alt du trenger å gjøre.

text: So then I remember in school one day when I seen, reading in my history, I was looking there, and no one was sitting there, and kiddies laughing at me, being so ragged, my hair hanging down my neck. And they would laugh at me. And I was reading a book where Abraham Lincoln got off of a boat down in New Orleans, and he was … he seen a colored man being auctioned off. He said, "That's wrong." He said, "That's wrong. And someday I will hit that. If I takes my life, I will hit it." And he did, and it took his life. Exactly. And I scooted my geography book … not mine, but one I had borrowed, I didn't have any of my own. I pushed it back, and I said, "And drinking is wrong, and someday I will hit if it takes my life." Against it? Yes, sir.
text_no:
Jeg husker en dag på skolen da jeg leste historie. Jeg satt der alene, mens de andre barna lo av meg fordi jeg var så fillete og hadde langt hår som hang nedover nakken. Der satt jeg og leste en bok om Abraham Lincoln, som en gang steg i land fra en båt i New Orleans. Han så en farget mann bli solgt på auksjon og sa: "Det er galt. Noe må gjøres med det, selv om det koster meg livet." Og det gjorde han, og det tok livet hans. 
Jeg skyvde fra meg geografiboken – ikke min egen, men en jeg hadde lånt fordi jeg ikke hadde noen selv. Jeg sa til meg selv: "Drikking er galt, og en dag vil jeg motarbeide det, selv om det koster meg livet." Mot det? Ja, herr.

text:
And I say this in regards right now, that any person that's really had a touch of Jesus Christ is finished with drinking. That's right.
I got my first Bible. People used to say, "Is it wrong to do this? Wrong to smoke? Wrong to drink?" I made … put a little slogan in the back of my Bible. I picked it up here a few days ago, and was looking at it, a little bitty old Bible. I said:
Don't ask me foolish questions,
Just make this up in your mind,
If you love the Lord with all your heart,
You don't smoke, chew, or drink any shine.
And that's right. That's still the thing to do and that's been twenty years ago since I wrote it in there. A man that's born again has no use for the stuff. Now, look what it has hatched out here in America. You can see whether there's any harm to it or not.
text_no:
Jeg sier nå dette: Enhver person som virkelig har rørt ved Jesus Kristus, har sluttet med å drikke. Det stemmer.
Jeg fikk min første Bibel. Folk pleide å spørre: "Er det galt å gjøre dette? Galt å røyke? Galt å drikke?" Jeg laget en liten setning og skrev den bak i Bibelen min. For noen dager siden plukket jeg opp denne gamle, lille Bibelen igjen og så på den. Jeg skrev:
Spør meg ikke dumme spørsmål,
Ta en beslutning,
Hvis du elsker Herren av hele ditt hjerte,
Røyker du ikke, tygger ikke, og drikker ikke.
Og det er riktig. Det er fortsatt den riktige holdningen, og det er tjue år siden jeg skrev dette ned. En mann som er født på ny har ikke behov for slike ting. Se nå hva dette har ført til her i Amerika. Du kan selv se om det er skadelig eller ikke.

text:
One time we had probation, 'course we had gang wars and things. But what did they do, just like fooling around with an egg: go to fool with the middle of it, you have the whole thing everywhere. And I'm not, I say I'm not a politician or nothing, it's none of my business what they do, that's their business. Mine is to preach the Gospel. But here's one thing, brother, that when … just as we went back and put whiskey in all these places, we took the prostitute off the role, and the drunken gambling places, and stuck right in our refrigerator.
I seen a picture one time of old John Barleycorn. They call him "the whiskey man." He had his hat sitting on the back of his head, and if he wasn't a horrible looking scarecrow. They painted him up now, they put him in bumpers, but he is still old John Barleycorn, the same old fellow. That's exactly right. It's like trying to paint a hog up, and wash him up, and try to make a good different creature out of him, he will go through the waller just as hard as he can go; till you get his nature changed.
text_no:
En gang hadde vi prøvetid, og selvfølgelig var det gjengkriger og slike ting. Men hva gjorde de? Det var som å leke med et egg: rører du ved midten, sprer det seg overalt. Jeg sier ikke at jeg er politiker eller noe slikt; det er ikke min sak hva de gjør, det er deres sak. Min oppgave er å forkynne Evangeliet. Men her er én ting, bror: da vi kom tilbake og satte whisky i alle disse stedene, tok vi bort prostitusjon og de fulle spilleplassene, og plasserte det rett i kjøleskapet vårt.
Jeg så et bilde av gamle John Barleycorn en gang. De kaller ham "whiskymannen." Han hadde hatten på bakhodet, og så ut som en skrekkslagen fugleskremsel. Nå har de malt ham opp og satt ham på bumpers, men han er fortsatt gamle John Barleycorn, den samme gamle fyren. Det er helt riktig. Det er som å prøve å pynte på en gris og vaske ham; han vil fortsatt gå rett tilbake til gjørmehullet så fort han kan, til du endrer naturen hans.

text:
So the thing that men and women has got to do now, is get their nature changed. God changes a man's makeup, changes his nature, makes him a new creature in Christ. I know you believe that.
Now, but, I never come here to preach, though I tell to you my life story. But just to think of how that those days, how that was…
I remember sitting in school. I went to school one complete year without a shirt on. I didn't even have a shirt to my name. Mrs. Watham, a rich woman, she's in Glory today, a Catholic woman, yet if … Oh, I know she was a Christian. And she give me a coat. And I wore that coat. I did … I had on an old pair of tennis shoes, and my feet was … the tops of them was out, and my toes stuck up like turtle heads out of a pond when … to see my feet sticking up, that snow coming down, and coming to school. I would sit there, and this big old coat on.
text_no:
Det folk må gjøre nå, er å få sin natur forandret. Gud forandrer en manns grunnleggende egenskaper, forvandler hans natur og gjør ham til en ny skapning i Kristus. Jeg vet at du tror på det.
Men jeg kom ikke hit for å forkynne, selv om jeg deler min livshistorie med dere. Bare tenk på hvordan det var den gangen…
Jeg husker at jeg satt på skolen. Jeg gikk et helt skoleår uten en skjorte. Jeg hadde ikke engang en skjorte til mitt navn. Fru Watham, en rik kvinne som er i Herlighet i dag, var katolikk, men jeg vet at hun var en kristen. Hun ga meg en frakk, og jeg bar den frakken. Jeg hadde på meg et gammelt par tennissko, og toppen av dem var utslitt, så tærne stakk ut som skilpaddehoder fra en dam. Jeg husker føttene mine stakk ut i snøen mens jeg gikk til skolen. Jeg satt der i klasserommet med denne store, gamle frakken på.

text:
It come spring of the year. And I remember one day, awful warm, and the perspiration just a running down my face. I thought, "My, it's hot!" Mrs. Temple, and she might be sitting present for all I know, she doesn't live too far from here. If she is, God bless you, mother Temple. She's been a lot to my life.
All right. What I'm going to say here … I called, maybe see if she is here, if you are, I still love you, sister. She said, "William." I had my coat collar buttoned up like this. She said, "William, aren't you hot with that coat on?" The kiddies begin to say, you know, and it didn't smell so very good, I suppose, wearing it all winter. And said, "Aren't you hot with that coat on?"
I said, "No, ma'am, I'm--I'm a little bit cold." Cold! I couldn't take that coat off, I didn't have no shirt on.
So she said, "Well, sonny, you must be taking a cold, William." She said, "You better come over to the stove."
So she built up the fire, sit me down there. And I sit there, and the perspiration just a pouring off of me. She said, "Aren't you warm enough to take that coat off yet, William?"
I said, "No, ma'am, Mrs. Temple. I'm still cold." I couldn't take it off, I didn't have no shirt on.
So she said, "Well, I believe you are sick. I better send you home." And she sent me home thinking that I was sick and I was cold, but I just didn't have on any shirt. I couldn't take it off.
text_no:
Det var vår. Jeg husker en dag som var uvanlig varm; svetten rant nedover ansiktet. Jeg tenkte: "Oj, så varmt det er!" Fru Temple, som kanskje er til stede her i dag, bor ikke langt unna. Hvis du er her, Gud velsigne deg, mor Temple. Du har betydd mye for meg.
Greit, det jeg vil si her... Jeg lurte på om hun er her, og hvis du er det, elsker jeg deg fortsatt, Søster. Hun sa: "William." Jeg hadde frakkekraven kneppet opp. Hun sa: "William, er du ikke varm med den frakken på?" Barna begynte å snakke, og den luktet nok ikke så godt etter å ha brukt den hele vinteren. Hun spurte igjen: "Er du ikke varm med den frakken på?"
Jeg svarte: "Nei, fru Temple, jeg er litt kald." Kald! Jeg kunne ikke ta av frakken, for jeg hadde ingen skjorte på under.
Hun sa: "Vel, lille venn, du må vel ha blitt forkjølet, William." Hun sa: "Du bør komme bort til ovnen."
Hun fyrte opp i ovnen og ba meg sette meg der. Jeg satt der, og svetten rant av meg. Hun sa: "Er du ikke varm nok til å ta av frakken nå, William?"
Jeg svarte: "Nei, fru Temple. Jeg er fortsatt kald." Jeg kunne ikke ta den av, da jeg ikke hadde noen skjorte på.
Hun sa: "Jeg tror du er syk. Jeg bør sende deg hjem." Så hun sendte meg hjem, overbevist om at jeg var syk og kald, men i realiteten hadde jeg bare ingen skjorte på under frakken og kunne derfor ikke ta den av.

text:
And I went to school with mama's shoe on one side, and papa's on the other one. That's exactly right, a boot-n-gegger [unclear words] if you know what I am talking about. Like a… And when great big boys, just because of Satan and sin.
And when we were eating, I remember, we couldn't eat with the rest of the kiddies. They would all have sandwiches, the light bread. You remember when used to have the old loaf bread that you would get it and save the tags off the back of it for certain things, safety razors and so forth? And I remember when they used to have that, and the women, most baked their bread. We couldn't do that. We couldn't afford it.
And they would all take sandwiches, and make little sandwiches. But brother, and I couldn't do that. We had this … We had a little half a gallon of molasses bucket, about like that. And in there, we had a little jar, and it would be full of greens, the next one full of beans, two pieces of cornbread, and two spoons. We would slip off. We was ashamed to eat before the other children who had cakes, and cookies, and things.
text_no:
Jeg gikk på skolen med mammas sko på den ene foten og pappas på den andre. Det stemmer helt, en "boot-n-gegger" [uklare ord], hvis dere skjønner hva jeg mener. Akkurat sånn… Og med de store guttene, bare på grunn av Satan og synd.
Jeg husker når vi spiste, vi kunne ikke spise sammen med de andre barna. De hadde alle sammen smørbrød med loff. Husker du de gamle brødene man kunne spare merkelappene fra for å bytte dem mot f.eks. barberblad? Jeg husker når de brukte å ha det, og de fleste kvinnene bakte sitt eget brød. Vi hadde ikke råd til det.
De andre barna lagde små smørbrød. Men min bror og jeg kunne ikke gjøre det. Vi hadde en liten halvgalons bøtte med sirup, omtrent sånn. I den hadde vi en liten krukke full av grønnsaker, en annen full av bønner, to stykker maisbrød og to skjeer. Vi snek oss unna fordi vi skammet oss over å spise foran de andre barna som hadde kaker og småkaker.

text:
And we would go down next to the river, and sit down there, and sit this out on the log, and sit there and eat, both of us. We … I'd take a bite out of the--out of the little jar of beans, and brother, take a bit. Then we would take a bite out of the greens. Not to much, we had to make it, divvy it up between us. And two pieces of cornbread, hoecake cornbread that mom had baked for breakfast, and cut in little slices like that, just had to go a long, with the rest of the kids.
Oh, I remember one time around Christmas time. I hate to get into these things. But around Christmas time, we had a Christmas tree. And the kids down at school would take and cut little white strips of paper, and blue ones, and green ones, and made little chains, you, know, how they used to do in school. And we took ours home. So mama thought, she went back out in the field, we did, and cut a little Christmas tree about like that.
text_no:
Vi pleide å gå ned til elven, sette oss på en tømmerstokk, og spise der sammen. Vi delte en liten krukke med bønner – jeg tok en bit, og bror tok en bit. Deretter delte vi litt grønnsaker. Vi måtte porsjonere det nøye, slik at vi begge fikk, inkludert to stykker maismuffin som mamma hadde bakt til frokost. De var kuttet i små skiver, slik at de måtte vare lenge med alle barna.
Jeg husker spesielt en gang rundt juletider. Jeg liker ikke alltid å snakke om dette, men rundt jul hadde vi et juletre. Barna på skolen klippet små hvite, blå og grønne papirstrimler og lagde kjeder, slik de pleide å gjøre. Vi tok dem med hjem. Så, mamma, tenkte hun, gikk ut på feltet, og vi skar ned et lite juletre.

text:
And papa went down. And he had got some popcorn that they had raised. And they popped the corn and made strings, and mom strung it up with a needle and thread to put around the Christmas tree where we was going to have a Christmas tree. We were hanging up our stockings on Christmas night, and next morning maybe have an orange, and three pieces of candy laying out, and a little piece of paper laying to one side, maybe little bitty pieces of candy.
And if we had an orange, and a piece of candy, and a apple, oh, what a great fellow Santa Claus was to come bring that to us. How happy we was. My, we would eat those oranges and dry the peeling and then eat the peeling. Many times I have packed peeling in my pocket for week after week and eat those orange peelings. Yeah, we wasted nothing of it.
text_no:
Pappa hadde fått litt popcorn som de hadde dyrket selv. De poppet popcornet og laget lenker, som mamma tredde med nål og tråd for å henge rundt juletreet vi skulle ha. Vi hang opp strømpene våre på julaften, og neste morgen kunne vi kanskje finne en appelsin, tre biter godteri og en liten papirlapp på siden, noen ganger med små godteribiter.
Hvis vi fikk en appelsin, et stykke godteri og et eple, syntes vi at julenissen var en fantastisk fyr som hadde brakt det til oss. Hvor glade vi ble! Vi spiste appelsinene, tørket skallet og spiste det også. Mange ganger har jeg hatt appelsinskall i lommen i ukevis og spist dem. Ja, vi kastet ingenting.

text:
And I remember very well one time when mama had popped some corn. She had another little half-a-gallon syrup bucket and she put that full of popcorn. And my brother that's in Glory today, when we took it down, set it in the old cloakroom, country school. And I got to sitting back there, and I thought, "Oh, what I would…" That was some … what we call a rarity, you know. My, it's something very rare. And I thought, "Wonder if I could just before dinner time get a good handful of that, see, before dinner time comes." So I fit it all up, so I raised up my hand, asked the teacher, "May I be excused?"
"Yes."
text_no:
Jeg husker veldig godt en gang da mamma hadde poppet noe mais. Hun hadde en annen liten bøtte på en halv gallon og fylte den med popcorn. Min bror som er i Herligheten i dag, tok den med ned til den gamle skolegarderoben på landsbyskolen. Jeg satte meg der bak og tenkte, "Å, hva jeg ville…" Det var noe vi kalte en sjeldenhet, du vet. Å, det var noe veldig uvanlig. Jeg tenkte, "Lurer på om jeg før lunsjtid kunne få en god håndfull av det, se, før lunsjtid kommer." Så jeg ordnet alt, rakte opp hånden og spurte læreren, "Kan jeg få gå ut?"
"Ja," svarte hun.

text:
And so we … I went out to the cloak room, I opened up this bucket, reached down in there and got a great big handful that corn. Put the bucket back, went around, the bucket lid, rather, went back and stood behind the old chimney back there, and eat that popcorn. Oh, it was good. I come back in and wipe my mouth real good, and my hands, you know, so my brother wouldn't notice it.
So when dinner time come, we went out, picked up our bucket, and went out to eat. After we'd… We won't eat the popcorn first, you know, because that was better than what we had. So we opened up the bucket, and about a third of it was gone. So my brother looked around, he said, "Say," he said, "something has happened to that popcorn."
I said, "Sure has." I knowed what had happened.
text_no:
Jeg gikk ut til garderoben, åpnet bøtta og tok en stor håndfull av maisen. Deretter satte jeg på lokket igjen og gikk bak den gamle skorsteinen for å spise popcornet. Det smakte fantastisk. Jeg kom tilbake, tørket munnen og hendene nøye slik at broren min ikke ville legge merke til det.
Da det ble tid for middag, hentet vi bøtta for å spise. Vi bestemte oss for å starte med popcornet, siden det var bedre enn det andre vi hadde. Da vi åpnet bøtta, så vi at omtrent en tredjedel var borte. Broren min kikket rundt og sa: "Hei, noe har skjedd med popcornet."
Jeg svarte: "Det har det virkelig." Jeg visste godt hva som hadde skjedd.

text:
And you know, friends, not long ago I come up from Houston, I was having a meeting there. And I had been so tired. I just couldn't, I would just pass out. I stayed eight days and nights without leaving the platform. I said, "I will pray for everybody comes." And I stayed there, and praying in the line till I was so unconscious, they packed me to the car. And I … they would…
I would lay against the pulpit and sleep a little, and then I would wake up, the prayer line still waiting. I don't know where it was out there on the street, I just keep on praying for one, the other. Then they would bring me something, I would eat a little bit, and then maybe pray till I would get so sleepy I would lean against the pulpit like that; for hours after hours. And I had gotten so worn, they tried to put me to bed, then I couldn't go to bed. Then I couldn't sleep.
text_no:
For ikke lenge siden kom jeg opp fra Houston etter å ha holdt et møte der. Jeg var så utmattet at jeg nesten besvimte. I åtte dager og netter forlot jeg ikke plattformen. Jeg sa: "Jeg vil be for alle som kommer." Jeg ble der og ba i bønnekøen til jeg var så utkjørt at de måtte bære meg til bilen.
Jeg lente meg mot prekestolen, sov litt, våknet og så at bønnekøen fortsatt ventet. Uten å vite omfanget ute på gaten, fortsatte jeg å be for den ene etter den andre. De brakte meg noe å spise, og jeg spiste litt før jeg ba videre til jeg ble så trøtt at jeg måtte lene meg mot prekestolen igjen; time etter time. Til slutt var jeg så utslitt at de prøvde å legge meg, men jeg klarte verken å legge meg eller sove.

text:
I started home. And I never will forget, on the road home, I was driving along and I would wake up. I had an old Ford. That has been about five years ago. And it was backslid and it was … Well, you know, what I mean, it was all right, it just been treated pretty heavy. And so, I didn't have any side in the thing where I beat my leg against it trying to keep awake, and pulled all the hairs till I don't have hair, just on the back of my hand, trying to keep awake, praying for the sick, trying to keep awake to make my lines go on.
I found somebody who loved me, somebody who loved me, and I loved them. And I was trying to minister my heart out to them. And I remember waking up. I… And the cars would be blowing, and I'd be asleep over on the other side of the road. And after a while, the funny part of it, I woke up, I stopped, I couldn't get to myself. And I had my hands out the window. And I was in a cow pasture, I had my hands out the window, saying, "Only believe, sister, that's the only thing you have to do. Just believe." And I said, "What's the matter with me?"
I got out. I done run off the road out into a cow pasture, asleep on the road. And I come home. And oh, my, when I got home, and there they was. And before, we kept the people from the house, and there they was lined up there, a hundred fifty, two hundred of them, sitting before the place.
text_no:
Jeg dro hjemover, og jeg glemmer aldri kjøreturen. Jeg kjørte en gammel Ford, dette var for omtrent fem år siden, og bilen var i dårlig stand. Du vet hva jeg mener, den var intakt, men hadde vært hardt brukt. Jeg hadde ikke noe polstring på siden, så jeg slo benet mot døren for å holde meg våken. Jeg dro ut alle hårene på baksiden av hånden min i et forsøk på å holde meg våken mens jeg bad for de syke, alt for å klare å kjøre videre.
Jeg fant noen som elsket meg, og som jeg elsket tilbake. Jeg prøvde å gi alt i min tjeneste til dem. Jeg husker at jeg våknet av at bilene tutet; jeg hadde sovnet på den andre siden av veien. En stund senere våknet jeg igjen, stoppet og innså at jeg var ute av meg selv. Jeg hadde hendene ut av vinduet og befant meg midt i en ku-eng, mens jeg sa: "Bare tro, søster, det er alt du trenger å gjøre. Bare tro." Jeg tenkte: "Hva er det som skjer med meg?"
Jeg gikk ut av bilen og så at jeg faktisk hadde kjørt av veien og inn i en ku-eng mens jeg sov. Da jeg kom hjem, så jeg at det var mange mennesker der. Tidligere hadde vi holdt folk borte fra huset, men nå satt det hundrevis utenfor, kanskje 150 eller 200.

text: Wife … I had prayed for as many as I possibly could, it was coming long toward daylight. And I heard her. Now, if there's many of these people might be here today. She got me to bed, and I was getting quiet. I'd wake up and after a while I would have my arm around a pillow, standing out in the floor, saying, "Now, who's next? Now if you will just believe. Jesus Christ said if I could get the people to believe," praying with my pillow in my arms.
text_no: Kone … Jeg hadde bedt for så mange jeg kunne, det nærmet seg daggry. Og jeg hørte henne. Nå, hvis mange av disse menneskene er her i dag. Hun fikk meg i seng, og jeg begynte å roe meg ned. Jeg våknet og etter en stund hadde jeg armen rundt en pute, stående på gulvet, og sa: "Nå, hvem er den neste? Nå hvis dere bare vil tro. Jesus Kristus sa at hvis jeg kunne få folk til å tro," ba jeg med puten i armene mine.

text:
And wife would sit down and cry. She is thirty-two years old, turned snow-white almost. If there is any credit goes to the Branham family, give it to my wife. She is the one deserves it, not me. And standing there, I remember, she…
I had just got asleep. I heard the rattling of a noise, and it was an old Chevrolet, drove all the way from up here in Ohio to come down. A little baby, crying, hadn't ceased for days. The doctor didn't know what was the matter with it. And I heard the wife say, "Now, if you will just sit down." It was long about, I guess three or four o'clock in the morning. Said, "If you will just sit down," said, "I will--I will fix you something to eat."
Said, "No, we have had breakfast, Sister Branham, but the only thing that … we just thought."
Said, "Well, we just got him to sleep." Said, "Don't wake him up right now."
And I was laying in there. And I hear that little baby going just like, you know, wheeze and funny noise, crying, till he just couldn't cry no more. Do you think I could sleep and that little thing laying in there, and think maybe a prayer would help it? I couldn't do it.
text_no:
Og min kone satt ned og gråt. Hun er trettito år gammel, og har nesten blitt helt hvithåret. Hvis noen ære skal gis til familien Branham, gi den til min kone. Det er hun som fortjener det, ikke jeg. Jeg husker at hun...
Jeg hadde nettopp sovnet. Jeg hørte lyden av noe raslende, og det var en gammel Chevrolet som hadde kjørt hele veien fra Ohio. En liten baby som hadde grått uten stopp i flere dager var med. Legen visste ikke hva som feilte den. Jeg hørte min kone si: "Nå, hvis dere bare vil sette dere ned." Klokken var omtrent tre eller fire om morgenen. Hun sa: "Hvis dere bare vil sette dere, så kan jeg lage noe mat til dere."
De svarte: "Nei, vi har hatt frokost, Søster Branham, men vi trodde bare..."
Min kone sa: "Vi har akkurat fått ham til å sove. Ikke vekke ham nå."
Jeg lå i sengen og hørte den lille babyen lage gispende og rare lyder, gråte til den nesten ikke kunne mer. Tror du jeg kunne sove med den lille som lå der, og tenke at kanskje en bønn kunne hjelpe? Det klarte jeg ikke.

text:
I staggered out into the room. And she started crying, went over and sat down. And I said, "Mother, do you believe?" And she… We have two little rooms where we were living. And she laid the baby up there on the table. And I said, "Let's kneel around the table." And we started praying.
While we were yet praying, the little baby quit crying. About an hour from then, they left. It was cooing and laughing to its mother. Went away, it was a little different.
She said, "Before the crowds get gathered in, let me take you somewhere." So we got out in the car, and went somewhere, up to Green's Mill, where I seen the vision, where I was commissioned at. We came back down along the evening. We passed by this old school house, where it used to stand. I stopped there.
I remember the old well that I used to drink from. And the kiddies was … little girl. My little Rebekah was picking some violets. She was just about a year old, or something, year an a half. And she was picking some violets out there, playing. And I went and drink from this old well. I thought, as David said, if he could just drink from that well.
text_no:
Jeg vaklet inn i rommet. Hun begynte å gråte, gikk bort og satte seg ned. Jeg sa, "Mor, tror du?" Og hun… Vi hadde to små rom der vi bodde. Hun la babyen opp på bordet. Jeg sa, "La oss knele rundt bordet." Vi begynte å be.
Mens vi ennå holdt på å be, sluttet den lille babyen å gråte. Omtrent en time senere dro de. Babyen pludret og lo til moren sin. Den var annerledes enn før.
Hun sa, "Før folkemengden samler seg, la meg ta deg med et sted." Så vi satte oss i bilen og kjørte til Green's Mill, der jeg hadde sett visjonen og fått mitt oppdrag. Vi kom tilbake på ettermiddagen og passerte den gamle skolen der den pleide å stå. Jeg stoppet der.
Jeg husket den gamle brønnen jeg pleide å drikke fra. Barna… Min lille jente, Rebekah, plukket noen fioler. Hun var bare rundt et år, eller noe sånt, ett og et halvt år. Hun plukket noen fioler der ute og lekte. Jeg gikk og drakk fra den gamle brønnen. Jeg tenkte, som David sa, hvis han bare kunne drikke fra den brønnen.

text:
And I went and leaned my arms over against the old wooden fence. I looked across there, I looked up across the field where I used to play. I remember out there how one day, dawn of the first, 1917, when a big snow come on the ground, there I remember all the boys with sleds, out riding. They could ride. Brother and I didn't have no sled.
I seen the old hill where we used to coast down. I didn't have no sled. You know what we used for a sled? We went down in the old country dump pile down there, and got an old dish pan. And I sit down, we would sit down in that dish pan, put our legs around one another. There was sleet on top the ground. Many of you remember the 1917 snow. And I would sit down in this dishpan, we would put our arms around on another, down the hill we would go, around, and around, and around, in a dish pan. We wasn't as much class as the rest of them, but we were riding just the same. So what difference did it make?
text_no:
Jeg lente meg over det gamle tregjerdet og så utover åkeren hvor jeg pleide å leke. Jeg husket spesielt en dag i 1917, da et stort snøfall dekket bakken. Alle guttene var ute med kjelker og moret seg. Broren min og jeg hadde ingen kjelke.
Jeg så opp mot den gamle bakken vi pleide å ake ned. Vi hadde ingen kjelke, men vet du hva vi brukte isteden? Vi gikk ned til den gamle søppelfyllingen og fant en gammel oppvaskbalje. Vi satte oss ned i den, med beina rundt hverandre. Det lå et lag med sludd på bakken. Mange av dere husker sikkert snøen i 1917. Vi satt oss i oppvaskbaljen, holdt rundt hverandre, og suste ned bakken, rundt og rundt. Vi hadde kanskje ikke like fine kjelker som de andre, men vi akte like fullt. Hva spilte det for noen rolle?

text:
We were riding down the hill in this old dish pan. And after a while, the bottom come out of it. I went and got me a log then, and we got on a log. And I remember coming down just above the hill. We had a little old log I had chopped with an axe, the front of it. And we would get down through there.
And there was a boy… That was the time of the World War I. Everybody wore a uniform that was able to put on one. And a boy friend of mine named Lloyd Ford, he used to sell these a pathfinders, and so he got him a boy scout suit. And oh, how I longed for a boy scout suit. My! And I would look at him in that boy scout suit. He would wear it to school, and how I liked that. I made an agreement with him, I said, "Lloyd, when you wear that thing out, would you give it to me?"
He said, "Sure, I will give it to you, Billy."
I said, "All right."
Well, on and on it went. And after while he quit wearing it. And I asked him about it. He said, "I will see what happened to it."
Well, the thing had been destroyed. The only thing he could find was one legging. So I asked him to bring me that. So he brought it to me.
text_no:
Vi kjørte ned bakken i en gammel vaskefat. Etter en stund falt bunnen ut av det. Jeg fant meg en tømmerstokk, og vi satte oss på den. Jeg husker at vi kom ned like over bakken. Vi hadde en liten tømmerstokk jeg hadde hugget med en øks foran. Vi fortsatte nedover med den.
Det var i tiden rundt første verdenskrig. Alle som kunne, bar uniform. En venn av meg, Lloyd Ford, solgte Pathfinders og fikk seg en speidersuit. Å, som jeg ønsket meg en speidersuit! Jeg så på ham i den speidersuiten, han brukte den til skolen, og jeg syntes det var flott. Jeg inngikk en avtale med ham: "Lloyd, når du har slitt ut den der, vil du gi den til meg?"
Han sa: "Sikkert, jeg skal gi den til deg, Billy."
Jeg sa: "Greit."
Tiden gikk, og etter en stund sluttet han å bruke den. Jeg spurte ham om den, og han sa at han skulle se hva som hadde skjedd. Det viste seg at den var ødelagt. Det eneste han kunne finne, var en leggvarmer. Jeg ba ham om å gi meg den, og det gjorde han.

text:
And I remember riding down the hill one day. I wanted to wear that legging so bad, I didn't know what to do. Coming down the hill one day, I had that legging stuffed in my coat. And I hit the bottom of the hill, and I raised up. And I said, "Oh, I hurt my leg!" I hadn't. I said, "Oh, my leg!" I said, "It just reminds me, you know, I've got one of my leggings to my boy scout suit!" I put that legging on. That was an excuse, you know. Here I was walking along with one legging on, you know.
And I went to the black board. You remember how you used to stand up at the old country schools, the black board, you know? Well, I got picked. I put this leg, the one that didn't have the legging, I already had it figured out, next to the board. And I put this, had the legging like that, so they couldn't tell I just had on one. I stood sideways like this, working the problems, see if everybody was watching that, one legging.
All the kiddies got laughing at me, and making fun of me and everything like that. And I started crying, teacher made me go home. There was my legging, you know.
And I always wanted to be a soldier. When I got old enough to go in the army… Of course, there was no war then, I remember when I was seventeen I signed up for the navy. My mother taken that out of me when I got home. Then when the next war come, why, they wouldn't have me.
text_no:
Jeg husker en dag da jeg syklet ned bakken. Jeg ønsket så sterkt å bruke den leggen, jeg visste ikke hva jeg skulle gjøre. På vei ned bakken, hadde jeg dyttet leggen i frakken min. Da jeg nådde bunnen av bakken, reiste jeg meg og sa: "Å, jeg har vondt i beinet!" Men jeg hadde ikke vondt. Jeg sa: "Å, beinet mitt!" Jeg sa: "Dette minner meg om at jeg har en av leggene til speideruniformen min!" Jeg tok på meg leggen. Det var bare en unnskyldning, du vet. Her gikk jeg med én legging på.
Jeg gikk til tavlen. Husker du hvordan vi pleide å stå ved tavlen på de gamle landsbyskolene? Vel, jeg ble valgt. Jeg hadde allerede planlagt det, så jeg stilte meg slik at det beinet uten legging var nærmest tavlen. Den andre leggen var skjult, slik at de ikke kunne se at jeg bare hadde på én. Jeg sto sidelengs og løste oppgavene, og alle barna begynte å le av meg og gjøre narr av meg. Læreren sendte meg hjem, og der var leggen min.
Jeg hadde alltid ønsket å bli soldat. Da jeg ble gammel nok til å gå inn i hæren, var det ingen krig. Jeg husker at da jeg var sytten, meldte jeg meg til marinen. Moren min fikk meg til å endre mening da jeg kom hjem. Da neste krig kom, ville de ikke ha meg.

text:
But you know what? I finally did join the army. You may not see my uniform, it's on the inside. I joined the Christian ranks of Jesus Christ to be a soldier of the cross. How thankful I am to wear that uniform this afternoon that represents Heaven, to join with the rest of you.
I was standing there looking at that, and thinking about those things as I was leaning across the fence. And I begin to think of brother, how I took that handful of popcorn from him. When we used to put our hands on one another's shoulders, stand there, and the flag would go up; the teacher, with that great big pointer, point, making us get in line. We would stand tramping like that, go into the school.
And I thought, "Well, look, you know, I used to remember Ralph Field. What happened to him?" Yep, he's gone. And I said, "There was Howard Higgins." Yep, he used to stand by me. What's happened to him? He got blowed up down at Colgate. I said, "Yes, that's right."
text_no:
Men vet du hva? Jeg meldte meg til slutt inn i hæren. Du ser kanskje ikke uniformen min, men den er på innsiden. Jeg sluttet meg til Jesu Kristi kristne rekker for å være en soldat for korset. Hvor takknemlig jeg er for å bære den uniformen i ettermiddag, som representerer Himmelen, og å være sammen med dere andre.
Jeg sto der og så på det, tenkte på disse tingene mens jeg lente meg over gjerdet. Jeg begynte å tenke på Bror, hvordan jeg tok den håndfullen popcorn fra ham.
