Da vi pleide å legge hendene på hverandres skuldre, stå der, og flagget ble heist; læreren, med den store pekestokken, fikk oss til å stille oss opp. Vi trampet av gårde til skolen.
Jeg tenkte: "Vel, se, jeg husker Ralph Field. Hva skjedde med ham?" Ja, han er borte. Jeg sa: "Der var Howard Higgins." Ja, han pleide å stå ved siden av meg. Hva skjedde med ham? Han ble sprengt i lufta nede på Colgate. Jeg sa: "Ja, det stemmer."

text:
I remember the different ones of what had happened to them. I said, "Now, my brother, Edward that stood right behind me and put his hand on my shoulder, the one I took the popcorn from," I said, "What happened to him?"
Years ago, he died calling for me, said, "Tell Billy," I wasn't a Christian yet, said, "tell Billy, that I love him and some day I will meet him in Heaven." I was… And I remember when the ranger rode out on the prairies and I climbed out of my saddle. Said, "Is your name Branham?"
I said, "Yes, sir."
He said, "William?"
And I said, "Yes, sir."
He said, "I have a message for you." And he handed it over to me, and I read the telegram: "Your brother, Edward, died last night." Hum. All that began to renew. And I was standing there looking across the fence, I could see that handful of popcorn.
Don't ever do nothing wrong, it will come back to you someday, no matter how little it is.
text_no:
Jeg husker de ulike hendelsene som skjedde med dem. Jeg sa, "Nå, min bror Edward, som sto rett bak meg og la hånden på skulderen min, han som jeg tok popkornet fra," jeg sa, "hva skjedde med ham?"
For mange år siden døde han mens han ropte etter meg. Han sa, "Fortell Billy," jeg var ennå ikke kristen da, "fortell Billy at jeg elsker ham, og at en dag vil jeg møte ham i Himmelen." Jeg var… Og jeg husker da rangereren kom ridende ut på prærien, og jeg steg av hesten. Han spurte, "Er ditt navn Branham?"
Jeg sa, "Ja, sir."
Han spurte, "William?"
Og jeg svarte, "Ja, sir."
Han sa, "Jeg har en beskjed til deg," og han ga meg telegrammet. Jeg leste: "Din bror Edward døde i går kveld.” Alt dette begynte å gjenoppfriskes i minnet. Jeg sto og så over gjerdet, og jeg kunne se den lille mengden popkorn.
Ikke gjør noe galt, det vil komme tilbake til deg en dag, uansett hvor lite det er.

text:
And I stood there, tears began to run down my cheek, I thought, "God, I would give the world, I would give the rest of my mortal life, if You will let me take that handful of popcorn and walk up to the door, and say, 'Edward, buddy, here's that handful of popcorn I cheated you out of that day.'" How I would give anything if I could have took it to him. But he's gone.
I looked up across the field where the old house used to stand up there. Why, there is a housing project. The spring is dry and gone.
I used to think of when we used … we had an old piece of a mirror that we drove nails around it in a tree, and a little old wash bench. When dad used come in there, about a hundred and sixty pounds, about five foot, seven or eight inches tall. Man? Oh, my, logger, muscles hanging on him like that. I can seen him roll them sleeves up, that old blue shirt, that old hickory shirt mom made herself, for him; rolled it up like that. When he would go to wash, and the muscles swelling back and forth, I would stand off, I said, "That's my dad. That's my dad. He will live a hundred years. That's my daddy. When I am an old man, I will still be patting my daddy with big muscles," see. He died at fifty-two. Here we have no continuing city, but we seek One to come.
text_no:
Jeg sto der, med tårer som rant nedover kinnene, og tenkte: "Gud, jeg ville gi hele verden, jeg ville gi resten av mitt jordiske liv, hvis Du lar meg ta den håndfullen med popcorn og gå til døren og si: 'Edward, kompis, her er den håndfullen med popcorn jeg jukset deg for den dagen.'" Jeg ville gi alt for å kunne gi det til ham. Men han er borte.
Jeg så oppover åkeren der det gamle huset pleide å stå. Nå er det et boligprosjekt der. Kilden er tørket ut og borte.
Jeg husket hvordan vi brukte et gammelt speilstykke som vi spikret fast i et tre, og en liten vaskebenk. Når far kom inn der, omkring hundre og seksti pund, omtrent fem fot, sju eller åtte tommer høy. Mann? Å, for en tømmerhogger, med musklene hengende som det. Jeg kunne se ham rulle opp ermene på den gamle blå skjorten, den gamle hickory-skjorten som mor laget selv til ham. Når han gikk for å vaske seg, og musklene svulmet fram og tilbake, ville jeg stå og si: "Det er min far. Det er min far. Han vil leve hundre år. Det er min pappa. Når jeg er en gammel mann, vil jeg fortsatt klappe min pappa med store muskler," ser du. Han døde i en alder av femtito år. Her har vi ingen varig by, men vi søker en som kommer.

text:
I knowed the old house there, was chinked up, and what a great house it was; torn and gone, a housing project. Where's that big fine bunch of boys? Nearly every one of them is gone.
I thought of Rolland Halloway a friend of mine. He used to stand there, little red-headed fellow, enough temper to fight a buzz saw, died in prison. He shot a man in a crap game.
I looked over here to Wilmer, thought what become of … Wilmer [unclear words]. I thought, "What happened to him?" Yes, that's right. What happened to him? He got into a knife fight with a fellow and he cut his throat with a knife.
I looked back over here and I seen … thought of Willis [unclear words] "What happened to you, Willis?" Yeah, I seen what happened to him, how he went out in a disease. It stripped his body.
text_no:
Jeg husket det gamle huset der, som var murt og hvilket flott hus det var; nå revet og borte, erstattet med et boligprosjekt. Hvor ble det av den store, fine gjengen med gutter? Nesten alle er borte nå.
Jeg tenkte på Rolland Halloway, en venn av meg. Han pleide å stå der, en liten rødhåret fyr med nok temperament til å kjempe mot en sirkelsag. Han døde i fengsel etter å ha skutt en mann i et terningspill.
Jeg så over til Wilmer og undret meg over hva som skjedde med ham. Ja, det stemmer. Han havnet i en knivkamp med en annen og fikk strupen skåret over.
Jeg så tilbake og tenkte på Willis. "Hva skjedde med deg, Willis?" Ja, jeg så hva som skjedde med ham. Han ble rammet av en sykdom som rev kroppen hans i stykker.

text:
I looked down yonder and seen each one. And I seem them all, and I thought, "O God, here I am left alone. Who am I? Where are they at?" The first thing you know, standing there, I was screaming out to the top of my voice, "O God, let the Angels of God come get this poor tired bulk, pack me away from here. This world is not my home any longer."
I just come out of that meeting where I had been mentally tore up for eight days and nights at the platform; I was shaking. And all those things running over. I thought, "Here we have no continuing city, but we are seeking One to come now." I thought, "O God." Wife come put her arm around me, Said, "Now look, honey, you come out here to rest, and here you are standing here crying like a baby. Don't do that."
I said, "Sweetheart, if you knew what was traveling through my heart and mind. I remember standing right here at that house when little Sharon took sick."
text_no:
Jeg så nedover og så hver eneste en. Jeg så dem alle, og tenkte, "O Gud, her er jeg igjen alene. Hvem er jeg? Hvor er de?" Før jeg visste ordet av det, sto jeg der og skrek ut så høyt jeg kunne, "O Gud, la Dine engler komme og hente denne trøtte kroppen, ta meg bort herfra. Denne verden er ikke lenger mitt hjem."
Jeg hadde nettopp kommet ut av et møte hvor jeg hadde vært mentalt utslitt i åtte dager og netter på plattformen; jeg skalv. Alle disse tankene raste gjennom hodet mitt. Jeg tenkte, "Her har vi ingen varig by, men vi søker en som skal komme nå." Jeg tenkte, "O Gud." Kona kom og la armen rundt meg og sa, "Se her, kjære, du kom ut hit for å hvile, og nå står du her og gråter som et barn. Ikke gjør det."
Jeg sa, "Kjære, om du bare visste hva som rørte seg i hjertet og sinnet mitt. Jeg husker at jeg sto akkurat her ved det huset da lille Sharon ble syk."

text:
She said, "Now don't think about that." I've got a real wife. And she called me away and picked up the baby and set it around my shoulders, and we went out in the car and drove away.
How, thinking of things. Sometimes you look, say, "Oh, Brother Branham, I bet…" You think, you don't know what's behind here brother. You don't know how many times this poor heart has been mashed, and crushed, and broke, and twisted. You don't understand it. That's right. It looks like a flowery bed of ease, but don't you think that Satan would let me get by like that?
It would take a week to stand here and tell you what's all the things happened, how I been right down to the age of death's door, then God would spare me. How Satan would set snares everywhere, and he's still got them set, going right down to the door. But he will not be able to take me till God's finished with me. Then I want to go when He's finished.
When I preach my last sermon, the Bible is closed for its last time on the pulpit, my last prayer has been offered up to God, I can't do no more for Him, then I want Him to come take me away. That's right.
text_no:
Hun sa, "Nå må du ikke tenke på det." Jeg har en ekte kone. Hun ropte på meg, tok opp babyen og plasserte den på skuldrene mine, og vi dro av gårde i bilen.
Når du tenker på ting, hender det at du ser det slik: "Å, Bror Branham, jeg vedder på …" Du tror du vet, men du kjenner ikke hele historien, bror. Du vet ikke hvor mange ganger dette stakkars hjertet har blitt knust, sønderrevet og vridd. Det forstår du ikke. Det er riktig. Det ser ut som en blomstereng av ro, men tro ikke at Satan lar meg slippe så lett unna.
Det ville tatt en uke å stå her og fortelle alt som har skjedd, hvordan jeg har vært helt ved dødens dør, før Gud skånte meg. Hvordan Satan stadig setter snarer, og han har dem fremdeles klare, rett ved døren. Men han kan ikke ta meg før Gud er ferdig med meg. Da ønsker jeg å gå når Han er ferdig.
Når jeg har forkynnet min siste preken, Bibelen er lukket for siste gang på prekestolen, og min siste bønn er sendt opp til Gud, når jeg ikke kan gjøre mer for Ham, da vil jeg at Han skal komme og ta meg bort. Det er riktig.

text:
As a boy, I had a very peculiar thing happen as a little lad. I was called one day after school, about seven years old, by an Angel, which told me never to drink, or to smoke, or to defile my body.
And I don't mean this to you sisters now, you see, but if there ever was a woman hater, I was one of them. My, I seen how they come when my daddy run that bootleg place. And I would see woman come there, young woman, with somebody else's husband. And the way they would carry on, I said, "If that's the way it is, I wouldn't have one of the varmints if they would [unclear words] to one of them. That's right. I … that's true, I thought it. I even…
The only respect I had for any woman was my mother. And that's right, and I knowed she was a lady. I seen her set on the door step with the babies in her arms, and cry, and cry, and cry because she was locked out of the house.
text_no:
Som gutt hadde jeg en veldig spesiell opplevelse da jeg var liten. En dag etter skolen, da jeg var rundt syv år gammel, ble jeg kalt av en Engel som fortalte meg at jeg aldri skulle drikke, røyke eller vanære kroppen min.
Og jeg mener ikke dette personlig mot dere søstre, men hvis det noen gang fantes en kvinneforakter, var jeg en av dem. Jeg så hvordan ting foregikk da min far drev et smugkro. Jeg så unge kvinner komme dit med andres ektemenn. Og måten de oppførte seg på, fikk meg til å tenke: "Hvis det er slik det er, ville jeg ikke hatt en av dem, selv om de tilbød seg." Det er sant, jeg tenkte virkelig slik.
Den eneste kvinnen jeg hadde respekt for, var min mor. Jeg visste at Hun var en ordentlig dame. Jeg så henne sitte på dørtappen med babyene i armene sine, gråte og gråte fordi hun var blitt låst ute av huset.

text:
When my dad, which was a real man when he was sober, but being drinking, how … what he would do. And I had a tough life to come up in.
I thought, "No, I won't have…" I didn't… When I was even seventeen, eighteen years old, I would pass down the street. And if I seen a girl that I [unclear words] to, I thought she was going to speak, not because … I just didn't want to have nothing to do it, wasn't twisting myself up with them. I went on the other side of the street. I didn't have nothing at all to do with it all. So I said, "I will…"
Here was my thoughts, "When I get to be of age, when my mother is well, the boys are settled down, and everything, and I can get enough money somewhere to help take care of my mother, I am going to Colorado, or to Washington State, or Canada, and I am going to be a trapper. I am going to get me a bunch of dogs, I am going to get me a bunch of traps, and I'm going to get my rifle, and Ill live there until I die, right in the mountains; trapping.
text_no:
Da jeg vokste opp, hadde jeg en tøff barndom. Faren min var en god mann når han var edru, men når han drakk, var det vanskelig. Jeg bestemte meg tidlig for at jeg ikke skulle la meg involvere i problemer.
Selv når jeg var 17 eller 18 år, unngikk jeg situasjoner som kunne føre meg på feil spor. Hvis jeg så en jente på gaten, og trodde hun skulle snakke til meg, gikk jeg over på den andre siden av gaten. Jeg ønsket ikke å få problemer eller bli involvert med noen.
Mine tanker var klare: "Når jeg blir voksen, når moren min har det bra, brødrene mine er etablert, og alt er ordnet, skal jeg samle opp nok penger til å ta vare på moren min. Så skal jeg dra til Colorado, Washington State eller Canada for å bli en fellefangstmann. Jeg skal skaffe meg noen hunder, feller og en rifle, og der skal jeg leve til jeg dør, midt i fjellene, mens jeg driver med fangst."

text:
My grandfather was a hunter, on my mother's side. And he … I was natured after him. And so, I said, "I just … that's what I am going to do." I had it in my mind. I said, "There isn't going to be any woman connected in it at all." So, isn't it funny how you can change your mind? Strange.
One day there was there was … as a boy, there was a little girl come along. And you know, teeth like pearls, eyes like a dove, neck like a swan, prettiest thing you ever seen. She looked at me, said, "How do you do, Billy?" That was it. Another…
She knew another boy, friend of mine, he told me, said, "Oh, she likes you."
I said, "Well, I kind of made a promise you know." Well, I was willing to give in.
text_no:
Min bestefar var jeger på morssiden. Jeg arvet hans interesser for jakt. Jeg sa til meg selv: "Det er dette jeg skal gjøre." Jeg var helt bestemt på det. Jeg sa: "Ingen kvinne skal være involvert overhodet." Merkelig hvordan man kan endre mening.
En dag, som gutt, møtte jeg en liten jente. Hun hadde tenner som perler, øyne som en due og en hals som en svane. Det vakreste synet jeg noensinne hadde sett. Hun så på meg og sa: "Hvordan har du det, Billy?" Og det var det. 
En venn av meg fortalte meg senere: "Hun liker deg."
Jeg svarte: "Vel, jeg hadde jo lovet meg selv noe." Men jeg var klar til å gi etter.

text:
And so he said, "I tell you, I will take my girl friend, and you take your girl friend," and said, "we will take them riding in my dad's old Ford," said, "if I can get the thing started." Said, "How much money can you rake up?"
I said, "I don't know." So we raked up enough to get two gallon of gasoline. We had about forty cents between us.
He said, "Now, we got to get them something, some soft drinks, or ice cream, or something."
So I said, "Well, you do the driving of the Ford, and I will do the buying." So I put the forty cents in my pocket. So he took … he was going to drive the Ford. And we got our old Ford and jacked the back wheel up, you know. And you know how you used to have to … spinning the crank? My, my. We got her started, and down the road we went, and got our girls.
Well, I sit in the back seat, you know. And my, I looked over there, thought, "You know, maybe they are not all that way." But … I was changing my mind. So, she would look over, she said, "It's pretty tonight, isn't it?"
I said, "Yes ma'am."
text_no:
Han sa: "Jeg forteller deg, jeg tar med meg kjæresten min, og du tar med deg kjæresten din," og sa: "Vi skal ta dem med på en tur i farens gamle Ford," sa han, "hvis jeg får startet den." Han sa: "Hvor mye penger kan du skrape sammen?"
Jeg sa: "Jeg vet ikke." Så vi skrapte sammen nok til å få to liter bensin. Vi hadde omtrent førti øre mellom oss.
Han sa: "Nå må vi få noe til dem, noen brus eller is, eller noe."
Så jeg sa: "Vel, du kjører Forden, så skal jeg kjøpe det." Jeg puttet de førti ørene i lomma. Han tok seg av kjøringen, og vi fikk gamle Forden i gang ved å jekke opp bakhjulet, vet du. Og du vet hvordan man måtte sveive i gang bilen på den tiden? Å, ja. Vi fikk den startet, og suste av gårde og hentet jentene våre.
Jeg satt i baksetet, vet du. Og jeg tittet bort på henne og tenkte: "Kanskje de ikke alle er slik." Jeg begynte å endre mening. Hun så bort på meg og sa: "Det er en fin kveld, ikke sant?"
Jeg sa: "Ja, frue."

text:
So we stopped at a little place, just about a square from where I live right now, a little old place called … a little old drive-in of affair. So I said … I … Jimmy Poole and I, we had it all made up what we was going to say, you know. And I said, "Jimmy, I am kind of thirsty." And I said, "Don't you think we ought to stop?"
And he said, "Yes." And so we pulled in. So, he says, he said, "I will go get it." And he didn't even have a dime, and I had his money, and I said, "Never mind, Jimmy. Just a minute, I will go get it," see.
So he and I go. Sandwich for a nickel, great big baloney sandwich for a nickel, you know, and got onion and everything on it. So we come back out. And I had some Cokes, you know. And oh, was we--was we somebody then. We sat there and drink these Cokes, you know, and eat these baloney sandwiches, the girls and all of us; we talked, you know.
And so, then, I went back to take the Cokes back, and it was just about the time that girls begin to act smart, begin to come smart alecks, smoking cigarettes. When I come back out, to my surprise, my little queen was smoking a cigarette. Well, I've always had my opinion of a woman who would smoke a cigarette, and I haven't changed it yet. It's the lowest thing she ever done. And that's right. It's just as bad as drinking.
text_no:
Vi stoppet ved et lite sted, bare et kvartal fra der jeg bor nå, et lite gammelt sted kalt ... et lite gammelt drive-in-sted. Jeg sa … Jimmy Poole og jeg, vi hadde allerede planlagt hva vi skulle si. Jeg sa, "Jimmy, jeg er litt tørst. Synes du ikke vi bør stoppe?"
Han svarte, "Ja." Så vi kjørte inn. Så sier han, "Jeg skal gå og hente det." Han hadde ikke en gang en tier, og jeg hadde pengene hans, så jeg sa, "Ikke tenk på det, Jimmy. Vent litt, jeg skal gå og hente det."
Så vi gikk sammen. En stor bologna-sandwich for en femmer, med løk og alt mulig. Vi kom tilbake ut. Jeg hadde med noen Coca Cola. Og, ja, følte vi oss spesielle da. Vi satt der og drakk disse Coca Colaene og spiste bologna-sandwicher, pratet med jentene og alt mulig.
Deretter gikk jeg tilbake for å levere glassene. Det var omtrent på den tiden jentene begynte å bli "smarte", oppføre seg som smartinger, og begynte å røyke sigaretter. Da jeg kom tilbake, til min overraskelse, var min lille dronning i ferd med å røyke en sigarett. Jeg har alltid hatt min mening om en kvinne som røyker sigaretter, og den har ikke endret seg. Det er det laveste hun kan gjøre. Det er like ille som å drikke.

text:
Go ahead, I see your faces getting red. But let me tell you something, let me tell … Mama… It will be good for you, it will help you. Now don't get up, I will know, and the rest of them will know you are guilty.
Look, let me tell you. Mama used to tell me … When I was a kid, we had to … we … to get our grease we would have to boil meat skins in a pan, you know. And we would have to take a lot of medicine, and every Saturday night, a bath, in an old cedar tub, and hold your nose and take castor oil; every Saturday night. I can't even stand the thoughts of the thing now.
And I used to hold my nose and gag, I said, "Oh, mama, please, don't. Please don't! Please don't. That big old spoon that old greasy looking stuff. Oh, mama, please don't, it makes me so sick."
text_no:
Gå videre, jeg ser at ansiktene deres blir røde. Men la meg fortelle dere noe, la meg si … Mor … Det vil være bra for dere, det vil hjelpe dere. Nå, ikke reis dere, jeg vil vite det, og resten vil vite at dere er skyldige.
Hør, la meg fortelle dere. Mor pleide å si til meg … Da jeg var barn, måtte vi … for å få fettet vårt måtte vi koke kjøttskinn i en panne, vet dere. Og vi måtte ta mye medisin, og hver lørdagskveld, et bad, i et gammelt sedertre-badekar, holde for nesen og ta lakserolje; hver lørdagskveld. Jeg kan knapt tåle tanken på det nå.
Og jeg pleide å holde for nesen og brekke meg, jeg sa, "Å, mor, vær så snill, ikke gjør det. Vær så snill, ikke. Vær så snill, ikke. Den store gamle skjeen med det der ekle, fetete greiene. Å, mor, vær så snill, ikke, det gjør meg så kvalm."

text:
She said, "If it don't make you sick, it don't do you any good." Maybe this will help you some too, make you right good and sick and you will stop it then. That's right. All right. She said…
And I remember, here sit my little girl sitting there smoking a cigarette. Oh, my! I kind of… She sure dropped in my estimation them. I said… Now she said … Begin to blow smoke like that, you know. And I thought, "If the good Lord wanted you to smoke He would have put a smokestack on you, see." And I looked over at her like that, "Uh-huh."
Looked in front, here was Jim's girl sitting there doing the same thing. Well, Jim smoked himself. So I looked around.
She said, "Will you--will you have a cigarette, Billy?"
I said, "No ma'am, thank you. I don't smoke."
She said, "You don't smoke?" Said, "Now, you just got through telling me that you didn't dance."
I said, "No, ma'am."
Said, "That you don't smoke?"
"No."
And she said, "Well, what do you like to do?"
I said, "I like to go fishing. I like to hunt." That didn't interest her."
So she said, "Well, you big sissy."
text_no:
Hun sa, "Hvis det ikke gjør deg dårlig, gjør det deg ingen nytte." Kanskje dette også vil hjelpe deg litt, gjøre deg skikkelig dårlig, så du slutter med det. Det stemmer. Greit. Hun sa…
Og jeg husker, her satt min lille jente der og røykte en sigarett. Å, min! Hun falt virkelig i min aktelse da. Jeg sa… Nå sa hun… Begynte å blåse røyk slik, vet du. Og jeg tenkte, "Hvis Herren ville at du skulle røyke, ville Han ha gitt deg en skorstein." Og jeg så på henne slik, "Uh-huh."
Jeg så foran meg, der satt Jims jente og gjorde det samme. Vel, Jim røykte også. Så jeg så meg rundt.
Hun sa, "Vil du--vil du ha en sigarett, Billy?"
Jeg sa, "Nei, frue, takk. Jeg røyker ikke."
Hun sa, "Røyker du ikke?" Sa, "Nå, du sa nettopp at du ikke danset."
Jeg sa, "Nei, frue."
Hun sa, "At du ikke røyker?"
"Nei."
Og hun sa, "Vel, hva liker du å gjøre?"
Jeg sa, "Jeg liker å fiske. Jeg liker å jakte." Det interesserte henne ikke.
Så hun sa, "Vel, din store svekling."

text:
A sissy! My daddy had called me that one time because I wouldn't take a drink of whiskey. And I wanted to, but there was Something wouldn't let me. So, I said, "What was that?"
And she said, "You are a big sissy."
And I said, "Give me them cigarettes."
And I took that cigarette just as intention to smoke it as I am to finish preaching this service this afternoon. I took it in my hand, trembling like that. I said, "Give me the material with it." And she give me the thing that you light it with, you know. And I got I all fixed like that, and I started to put it in my mouth, shaking like that, and I heard Something going, "Whoooosh." And I stopped, and I looked around, I thought, "Now that wasn't right?"
She said, "What's the matter?"
I said, "Nothing-nothing." I said, I--I'm--I just trying to light it." And I started up to my mouth again.
You heard me tell my story the other night, how that whirl in the bush back there. There it was repeating again, "Whooooosh." I dropped the cigarette, I started crying.
She said, "Now I know you are a sissy."
text_no:
En pyse! Pappa kalte meg det en gang fordi jeg ikke ville ta en drink med whiskey. Og jeg ønsket å gjøre det, men det var Noe som hindret meg. Så jeg spurte: "Hva var det?"
Hun sa: "Du er en stor pyse."
Jeg svarte: "Gi meg de sigarettene."
Jeg tok sigaretten med intensjonen om å røyke den, like bestemt som jeg er på å fullføre denne prekenen i ettermiddag. Jeg tok den i hånden, skjelvende, og sa: "Gi meg det du tenner den med." Hun ga meg tennmaterialet, og jeg fikk alt klart. Da jeg skulle putte sigaretten i munnen, hørte jeg noe som gikk, "Whoooosh." Jeg stoppet og kikket rundt og tenkte: "Det der var ikke riktig."
Hun spurte: "Hva er i veien?"
Jeg sa: "Ingenting, ingenting. Jeg prøver bare å tenne den." Jeg førte den mot munnen igjen.
Dere hørte meg fortelle historien min tidligere, om den hvirvelvinden i busken der bak. Det gjentok seg: "Whooooosh." Jeg slapp sigaretten og begynte å gråte.
Hun sa: "Nå vet jeg at du er en pyse."

text:
I was… Closed that little old tin door on the Ford, and started up the road crying. Jim drove along in front, said, "Come on, get in, Bill." I said, "Nope, no."
I started walking up the road, she said, "Why, Billy," she said, "you great big sissy, you." Said, "I thought you was a man."
I said, "I thought I was too." And I just went on up the road like that; walking.
I cut across the fields, went up there and sat down in the field, and I said, "Oh, if there was some way that I could die here. Nobody wants me. I'm not fit for nobody." I said, "And the boys, they all like to go to dances and big times, and the girls like to smoke cigarettes, and here I am with … slave of circumstance. What I--what's the use … what's for me in life? What do I live for?" And I sat there in that field and cried till nearly daylight. On down…
text_no:
Jeg lukket den lille blikkdøra på Ford-en og begynte å gå opp veien mens jeg gråt. Jim kjørte foran og ropte: "Kom igjen, sett deg inn, Bill." Jeg svarte: "Nei, det går bra."
Jeg fortsatte å gå opp veien da hun sa: "Hvorfor, Billy, du er en stor pyse, er du ikke?" og la til: "Jeg trodde du var en mann."
Jeg svarte: "Det trodde jeg også." Og så fortsatte jeg bare å gå.
Jeg krysset over jordene, gikk opp der og satte meg ned. Jeg sa: "Å, om det fantes en måte jeg kunne dø her på. Ingen vil ha meg. Jeg duger ikke for noen." Jeg fortsatte: "Gutta liker å gå på dans og ha det moro, og jentene liker å røyke sigaretter. Og her er jeg ... en slave av omstendighetene. Hva er vitsen? Hva er det for meg i livet? Hva lever jeg for?" Jeg satt der i åkeren og gråt til det nesten ble dagslys.

text:
I have to hurry to get out of here in time that I promised you, just skipping the high places.
I guess you wondered how I ever got married if I was that bashful; backwards. I…
Finally I met a girl was my boys' mother. If there ever was an angel, that was her. I love her yet. She was a lovely girl. I met her, she was going to church. I looked at her, there was something different from anyone else. I knowed nothing about Christianity; I was already about twenty-one years old. I looked at her, she seemed to be every speck of a lady, the way she carried herself, and the respect she had. She was going to a Baptist church.
And I went out with her and started going with her. And I was the … went to work for the public utilities of Indiana. And I had gotten a hold of a little money and I had bought me an old car, and I thought, "Well, that was a real opportunity."
text_no:
Jeg må skynde meg for å komme meg herfra innen den tiden jeg lovet deg, så jeg hopper over de mindre viktige delene.
Du lurer sikkert på hvordan jeg i det hele tatt klarte å gifte meg hvis jeg var så sjenert og tilbaketrukket. Jeg...
Til slutt møtte jeg en jente som ble mor til mine gutter. Hvis noen noensinne har vært en engel, var det henne. Jeg elsker henne fortsatt. Hun var en fantastisk jente. Da jeg møtte henne, var hun på vei til menigheten. Jeg så på henne, og det var noe annerledes med henne enn noen andre. Jeg visste ingenting om kristendom; jeg var allerede rundt tjueen år gammel. Jeg så på henne, hun fremsto som en ekte dame, måten hun bar seg selv på og respekten hun hadde. Hun gikk til en baptistmenighet.
Jeg begynte å gå ut med henne og fulgte henne til menigheten. Jeg begynte å jobbe for de offentlige forsyningene i Indiana. Jeg hadde fått tak i litt penger og kjøpt meg en eldre bil. Jeg tenkte, "Det var en virkelig mulighet."

text:
But her father was the President of the Brotherhood on the Pennsylvania Railroader. Many of you railroaders here might know him, Charlie Brumbach; just recently went to Glory. And a very … had a good job. And he made about five hundred dollars a month. I was making about twenty cents an hour in a ditch, digging. And me go with a girl like that, I thought, "Uh-oh, something wrong here."
So, I went with her for while and I seen that she was every bit of a lady. And I knowed I had to make my choice now. I couldn't let … take that girl's time. I loved her too much for that, that I couldn't take her time, to me, because it wouldn't be right to spoil her life like that. It … I thought enough of her if I…
text_no:
Men hennes far var president for Brorskapet på Pennsylvania Jernbane. Mange av dere som jobber på jernbanen her, kjenner kanskje til ham, Charlie Brumbach; han gikk nylig bort til Herligheten. Han hadde en veldig god jobb og tjente omtrent fem hundre dollar i måneden. Jeg tjente omtrent tjue cent i timen med grøftegraving. Å være sammen med en sånn jente fikk meg til å tenke, "Uh-oh, noe er galt her."
Jeg var sammen med henne en stund og så at hun var en virkelig anstendig dame. Jeg visste at jeg måtte ta et valg nå. Jeg kunne ikke la henne kaste bort tiden med meg. Jeg elsket henne for mye til å ta hennes tid fordi det ville ikke være riktig å ødelegge livet hennes slik. Jeg tenkte nok på henne til å …

text:
As poor as I was, and I didn't have no dad at that time and so forth, and ten children to take care of, and … Dad left her nine, ten, with myself. And I thought, "How, then would I--would I ever be able to support someone like that?" And I thought, "I've got to make up my mind. I've got to either ask her … to marry her, or I've got to let her go, and let some good boy that will take a hold and she will go with him, and marry her, and make her a good home and everything, and she would be happy.
And along in that time, I begin to study. And I just … While I was going with her I had come to Christ and had found Him as my Savior, and was studying in the ministry; the Baptist church. Then, a little … an old … time kept going on and I was ordained then as a local elder, the exhorter. Then they had my ministerial license. And I thought, "Maybe, if I go to preaching altogether, could I make her a living?"
text_no:
Til tross for at jeg var fattig, og uten en far på den tiden, med ti barn å ta vare på, tenkte jeg, "Hvordan skulle jeg kunne klare å forsørge noen som henne?" Far hadde forlatt oss med ni barn, pluss meg selv. Jeg visste at jeg enten måtte be henne om å gifte seg med meg, eller la henne gå slik at en god gutt kunne komme og gi henne et godt hjem og alt hun trengte for å være lykkelig.
I løpet av den perioden begynte jeg å gruble mye. Mens jeg gikk ut med henne, hadde jeg kommet til Kristus, funnet Ham som min Frelser, og studerte for å bli forkynner i den baptistiske menigheten. Etter hvert ble jeg ordinert som lokal eldste og fikk min forkynnerlisens. Jeg tenkte, "Kan jeg forsørge henne hvis jeg blir predikant på heltid?"

text:
So one day, I thought, "I believe…" Made up my mind, I was going to ask her if she … [Blank spot on tape.] how was I going to do it. That was the big problem, how was I going to ask her to marry me? So I said, "Well, I will ask her tonight."
Well, I would go up, you know and I would talk, and when I would get right down to that spot, I just wilt away, I couldn't do it. I couldn't ask her to marry me, there was too many circumstances there. And I said … So I thought, "Well, how in the world will I ever get it over to her? Maybe I could ask somebody else to ask her if she would marry me, you see." I thought, "That wouldn't be just exactly right. She might refuse me on them terms."
text_no:
En dag tenkte jeg, "Jeg tror ..." Jeg bestemte meg for at jeg skulle spørre henne om hun ... [Tomt.område.på.lydbånd.] hvordan jeg skulle gjøre det. Det var det store problemet: hvordan skulle jeg spørre henne om å gifte seg med meg? Så jeg sa, "Vel, jeg skal spørre henne i kveld."
Jeg gikk opp, du vet, og jeg snakket med henne. Når jeg kom til det punktet hvor jeg skulle spørre, trakk jeg meg tilbake. Jeg kunne ikke gjøre det. Jeg kunne ikke spørre henne om å gifte seg med meg; det var for mange omstendigheter. Så tenkte jeg, "Hvordan i all verden skal jeg få det til? Kanskje jeg kunne be noen andre spørre henne om hun ville gifte seg med meg." Men så tenkte jeg, "Det ville ikke være riktig. Hun kunne kanskje avslå under de vilkårene."

text:
So you know how I done it? I wrote her a letter and asked her if she would. So I wrote a letter. And now it wasn't 'Dear Miss,' you know, it had a little more than that. It wasn't a business letter, yet it was in one way. But I wrote and told her how much I thought of her, and asked her if she would--she would marry me.
And then I thought I would just hand it to her some night. And I thought, "No, I believe I will put it in the mail." So I put me a stamp on it and I was going to work, and I stuck it in the mail box. I was to meet her on Wednesday, and that was on a Monday morning. So I wrote the letter, and put in the mailbox, went on to work.
text_no:
Så, vet du hvordan jeg gjorde det? Jeg skrev et brev til henne og spurte om hun ville gifte seg med meg. Brevet var ikke bare et formelt "Kjære frøken"; det inneholdt litt mer enn det. Det var ikke en forretningsbrev, men samtidig var det litt det. Jeg skrev og fortalte henne hvor mye jeg tenkte på henne, og spurte om hun ville gifte seg med meg.
Så tenkte jeg at jeg bare skulle gi henne brevet en kveld. Men så ombestemte jeg meg og bestemte meg for å sende det i posten. Jeg tok et frimerke og limte det på konvolutten før jeg dro på jobb, og la det i postboksen. Jeg skulle møte henne på onsdag, og dette var på en mandag morgen. Så jeg skrev brevet, la det i postboksen, og dro på jobb.

text:
And all that week I was waiting for Wednesday to come to go get my girl friend. We was going to church. So that night, I remember, when I started up towards the place to where her people lived … they lived in a lovely big home up there. And I thought, "And here I lived. Oh, my! And I thought … Well, I drove up in front. And I thought…
I knowed better than to blow the horn. I know her mother and dad would both be out on me. And I think that is right. That's cheap for you boys to go up blow the horn for the girl to come out. If you don't think enough of her to go in and talk to her, and bring her out, and talk to her mother and dad, you ain't … you oughtn't to be with her anyhow. That's right. Go, be a man.
So I walked up on the door, and I thought, "I will stay outside tonight." And I happened to get to thinking.
text_no:
Hele den uken ventet jeg spent på onsdag, for da skulle jeg hente kjæresten min. Vi skulle i menigheten. Den kvelden husker jeg godt, da jeg gikk mot stedet der foreldrene hennes bodde. De bodde i et vakkert, stort hjem der oppe, mens jeg bodde enklere. Jeg kjørte opp foran huset og tenkte ...
Jeg visste bedre enn å bruke bilhornet. Jeg visste at både moren og faren hennes ville bli misfornøyde. Og jeg mener det er riktig. Det er respektløst av unge menn å tute på hornet for å få jenta til å komme ut. Hvis du ikke har nok respekt for henne til å gå inn, snakke med hennes foreldre, og hente henne selv, burde du kanskje ikke være sammen med henne i det hele tatt. Det er riktig. Vær en mann.
Så jeg gikk opp til døren, og tenkte, "I kveld blir jeg stående utenfor." Da begynte jeg å tenke.

text:
Now her father was, he was one of the finest man, and her mother is a good woman, and I'm not too sure, she might be sitting here this afternoon, see. We don't live far from here. And if I say anything wrong, now Mrs. Brumbach, I don't mean to hurt your feelings, but I just want to tell this truth, you see. So if…
So I remember we were--we were … I went up on the porch.
Her mother, at that time … She likes me now, but she didn't care to much about me. And she was raised in one of those society churches, you know, that stands up, "Uh-huh, " the doxology and oh, my. You know all that there is going on. Well, that was just a little bit too much, I couldn't digest that. So I … She thought that I was a just a little bit narrow-minded, I guess.
text_no:
Hennes far var en av de beste mennene, og moren hennes er en god kvinne, og jeg er ikke helt sikker, men hun kan være til stede her i ettermiddag. Vi bor ikke langt herfra. Og hvis jeg sier noe galt, Mrs. Brumbach, så mener jeg ikke å såre følelsene dine, men jeg vil bare fortelle sannheten.
Jeg husker at vi var... jeg gikk opp på verandaen. Moren hennes, på den tiden... Hun liker meg nå, men hun brydde seg ikke så mye om meg da. Hun var oppvokst i en av de der sosietetsmenighetene, du vet, de som står opp, "Uh-huh," og synger doksologi, og å, min. Du vet alt som foregår der. Vel, det var bare litt for mye for meg; jeg klarte ikke helt å fordøye det. Så hun syntes nok at jeg var litt sneversynt.

text:
So I thought now, happened to go to thinking, "What…" before I got to the house, "what if her mother happened to get a hold of that letter and read it, then what would happen?" Oh my! And you know the devil is there to make me believe that she got the letter. So I said, "Oh, what will I do if she got that letter?" Hum!
I thought, "You know what the best thing for me to do, instead of ringing the doorbell tonight, I believe I will knock on the door and just leave my Ford sit with the door open, see, because I was going to get away from there."
And I can just hear her say, "William Branham!" mother and daddy, who was a fine Dutchman. And so I went up to the door, and I knocked on the door, and the first thing you know, here come Hope to the door. Her name was Hope. And so I… She came to the door, she said, "Hello, Billy."
And I said, "Evening."
She said, "Won't you come in?"
I thought, "Uh-oh, you get me in there where your mother is at now, and you both been reading that letter. No."
I said, "Thank you. It's very warm," I said, "I will just sit on the porch."
text_no:
Så jeg begynte å tenke, "Hva vil skje...," før jeg nådde huset, "hva om moren hennes fikk tak i det brevet og leste det, hva ville skje da?" Å, for en tanke! Og du vet, djevelen var der for å få meg til å tro at hun hadde fått brevet. Så jeg sa, "Hva gjør jeg hvis hun fikk det brevet?" Hm!
Jeg tenkte, "Du vet, det beste for meg å gjøre, i stedet for å ringe på dørklokken i kveld, er å banke på døren og la Ford-en stå med døren åpen." Jeg var klar til å komme meg vekk derfra raskt.
Jeg kunne bare se for meg at hun sa: "William Branham!" Moren og faren hennes, som var en flott nederlender, ville rope slik. Så jeg gikk opp til døren og banket på. Før jeg visste ordet av det, kom Hope til døren. Navnet hennes var Hope. Hun åpnet døren og sa: "Hei, Billy."
Jeg svarte: "God kveld."
Hun sa: "Vil du komme inn?"
Jeg tenkte, "Å nei, hvis jeg kommer inn der hvor moren din er, og dere begge har lest det brevet, nei takk."
Jeg sa, "Takk, men det er veldig varmt. Jeg setter meg heller på verandaen."

text:
She said, "Oh, step in." Said, "Mother and dad wants to see you." And oh, my! I knew then it was up. I thought, "Here it is, 'Won't you step in?'"
And I said, "Well, Hmm!" I thought, "Oh, my, I know it's over now." So I said, "Thank you."
I stepped in, took my hat off, and stood at the door. She said, "Come on out in the kitchen where mother and dad is," said, "I will be ready just in a few minutes."
And I thought, "Oh!" I walked out, I said, "How do you do, Mr. Brumbach! How do you do, Mrs. Brumbach?"
Said, "Hello, Billy. Won't you come out and have a glass of ice tea?"
I said, "Thank you," I said, "I will--I will sit in here if you don't mind."
"No, come out and sit down."
text_no:
Hun sa: "Å, kom inn." Og la til, "Mor og far vil gjerne se deg." Da visste jeg at noe var på gang. Jeg tenkte: "Her er det, 'Vil du komme inn?'"
Jeg sa: "Vel, hmm!" Jeg tenkte, "Å nei, nå er det slutt." Så jeg sa: "Takk."
Jeg gikk inn, tok av meg hatten og ble stående ved døren. Hun sa, "Kom ut på kjøkkenet, her er mor og far," og fortsatte, "Jeg er klar om noen få minutter."
Jeg tenkte: "Å!" og gikk inn. "Hvordan står det til, Mr. Brumbach? Og med deg, Mrs. Brumbach?"
De sa: "Hei, Billy. Vil du ha et glass iste?"
Jeg svarte: "Takk," og la til: "Jeg kan sitte her inne, hvis det er greit."
"Nei, kom ut og sett deg ned," sa de.

text:
I thought, "Oh, my!" My heart was just a jumping as hard as it could. A few minutes … I began to see then. They never mentioned it, they was talking about something else. I thought, "She never got the letter. It's all right." Well, then I thought…
Now the next thing, we better get to church. And so that night, Hope said, "Let's walk down to the church."
And I said, "Uh-oh!"
So that night we walked on down to the church and went in. I never did hear nothing Dr. Davis said, He was preaching a good sermon, but I was sitting there wondering, I thought, "Boy, she got that letter. The reason she wanted me to walk is because she is going to tell me this is my last night, see. I know it. And I was sitting there looking at her. I thought "Oh, I hate to give her up. My, but I guess she is right because I couldn't--I couldn't make her a living like her daddy could, and there it is." And I said, "She's got that letter."
text_no:
Jeg tenkte: "Å, kjære!". Hjertet mitt banket så hardt som det kunne. Etter noen minutter begynte jeg å se det. De nevnte det aldri; de snakket om noe annet. Jeg tenkte: "Hun fikk aldri brevet. Det er i orden." Så tenkte jeg...
Neste ting, vi burde komme oss til menigheten. Den kvelden sa Hope: "La oss gå ned til menigheten."
Jeg sa: "Å nei!"
Så den kvelden gikk vi ned til menigheten og gikk inn. Jeg hørte ikke mye av hva Dr. Davis sa. Han holdt en god preken, men jeg satt der og undret. Jeg tenkte: "Hun har fått det brevet. Grunnen til at hun ville gå, er fordi hun skal fortelle meg at dette er min siste kveld, se. Jeg vet det." Og jeg satt og så på henne. Jeg tenkte: "Å, jeg hater å gi henne opp. Men jeg antar at hun har rett, for jeg kunne ikke—jeg kunne ikke gi henne et liv som faren hennes kunne, og sånn er det." Og jeg sa: "Hun har fått det brevet."

text:
And oh, my, I never heard nothing the preacher said. I just sit there wondering. And oh, I look at her, and she looked more beautiful than she ever did, and I knowed she was every wit a lady. And I thought the woman that… She doesn't smoke, she doesn't go to dances, she doesn't have … she doesn't use any kind of bad talk. She's just--she's just an angel. And I thought, "My, that was her, but I guess it is all over now."
So after church was over, I started going home, you know, walking along, she was walking along. And I was looking up, when we would pass under the trees, the moonlight come down upon her black hair, and her brown eyes. I thought, "Oh, my, isn't she pretty?" Walking along. I thought…
Well, we begin to get kind of close to the house, and I got brave. I thought, "The letter hung up the box, none of them got it, see." I was just feeling pretty good, you know. I said, "Nobody got that letter so I'm all right. Say…" Going on like that.
text_no:
Jeg hørte ikke et ord av det forkynneren sa. Jeg satt bare der og undret meg. Jeg så på henne, og hun var vakrere enn noen gang. Jeg visste at hun var en ekte dame. Jeg tenkte på hvordan hun ikke røykte, ikke gikk på danser, ikke brukte upassende språk. Hun var som en engel. Jeg tenkte, "Det var henne, men nå er det nok over."
Etter menigheten var ferdig, begynte jeg å gå hjemover. Vi gikk ved siden av hverandre. Måneskinnet gjennom trærne falt på hennes svarte hår og brune øyne. Jeg tenkte, "Å, så vakker hun er." 
Da vi nærmet oss huset, følte jeg meg modigere. Jeg tenkte, "Brevet ble hengende i boksen, ingen av dem fikk det." Jeg følte meg ganske bra. Jeg sa, "Ingen fikk tak i det brevet, så alt er i orden."

text:
And she was talking, you know. And I reached over and took a hold of her arm, you know, was walking along. Oh, my. And I thought, "I have a little more grace. That letter, I hope it did hang up and there wasn't nothing happened to it." And I was done made up my mind then that if she knowed anything about it, she done said something about it."
So we was getting pretty close to home, directly she looked down to me, she said, "Billy?"
And I said, "Yes."
She said, "I got your letter."
Oh, I felt something move up and go down, you know. I said, "You did?"
She said, "Uh-huh." Just kept on walking, never said a word.
I thought, "Woman, say something before I faint. Do something, now. I can't sit like this all the time." We was getting to close to the house, she never said a word. I thought, "Well, I … say something."
text_no:
Hun snakket, vet du. Jeg tok tak i armen hennes mens vi gikk sammen. Å, min. Jeg tenkte, "Jeg har litt mer nåde. Jeg håper det brevet ikke skapte noen problemer." Jeg hadde allerede bestemt meg for at hvis hun visste noe om det, ville hun ha sagt noe.
Da vi nærmet oss hjemmet, så hun på meg og sa, "Billy?"
Jeg svarte, "Ja."
Hun sa, "Jeg fikk brevet ditt."
Jeg følte noe røre på seg inne i meg og synke igjen. Jeg spurte, "Gjorde du det?"
Hun svarte bekreftende og fortsatte å gå uten å si noe mer. Jeg tenkte, "Kvinne, si noe før jeg besvimer. Gjør noe nå. Jeg kan ikke stå i denne spenningen." Vi var veldig nær huset, og hun hadde fortsatt ikke sagt noe. Jeg tenkte, "Vel, si noe."

text:
She just … You know how woman can keep you. Excuse me. No, no, I mean--I mean, you know what I mean. So, never said a word, just walked along, you know, looking along up towards the moon and the stars. Oh, my, such suspense.
And I said, "Did you read it?"
She said, "Uh-huh." Kept walking on. That was all I could get out of her.
Well, I thought, "My, my, now what?" I said, "Did you appreciate it?"
She said, "Uh-huh." That was all I could get out of her, just "Uh-huh."
Well, we got married. So there you are. We got married.
And I never will forget, she asked me just before we was … when we was … before I got her a ring. And I remember, I paid eight dollars for the set.
text_no:
Du vet hvordan kvinner kan være. Unnskyld meg. Nei, nei, jeg mener--du vet hva jeg mener. Så, jeg sa aldri et ord, bare gikk bortover, du vet, og kikket opp mot månen og stjernene. Åh, for en spenning.
Jeg spurte, "Leste du det?"
Hun svarte, "Uh-huh," og fortsatte å gå. Det var alt jeg fikk ut av henne.
Jeg tenkte, "Åh, hva nå?" Jeg spurte, "Likte du det?"
Hun svarte, "Uh-huh." Igjen, det var alt jeg fikk ut av henne, bare "Uh-huh."
Vel, vi giftet oss. Så der er vi. Vi giftet oss.
Jeg glemmer aldri da hun spurte meg, rett før vi skulle … før jeg ga henne en ring. Jeg husker at jeg betalte åtte dollar for settet.

text:
And so, I was very happy about it though. My, I remember we drove out there under the tree, and I put that engagement ring on her finger, how happy I was. And had the other one down in my pocket and put me a great big catch pen there so it wouldn't get out. I was keeping her right there, boy. That one, she was going got be mine.
So I went on, you know. And she said, "Billy." Before I put the ring on her finger, she said, "don't you think it would be kind of gentleman-like if you would ask dad and mother?"
I thought, "Oh, my! Here it goes again." And she said … I said, "Yes." I said, "Look, Hope, I want to tell you something." I said, "Now when we get married, it's always to be a fifty-fifty, isn't it?"
She said, "That's right." She said, "I will keep my part."
I said, "I will mine." I said, "Let's start it now. See?"
She said, "What do you mean?"
I said, "You ask your mother, and I will ask your daddy." I could get by with her dad, but I didn't know about her mother.
She said, "All right. That's all right."
And I said, "Well, look," I said, "perhaps you let me ask your dad, first." Because I knowed if her dad said so, I had that much of a promise, see, and I could hold to that.
So, I remember she said, "Well, you better ask him tonight."
And I thought, "Oh, kind of quick, but I guess I better."
text_no:
Jeg var veldig glad for det. Å, jeg husker vi kjørte ut under treet, og jeg satte forlovelsesringen på fingeren hennes – hvor lykkelig jeg var. Jeg hadde en annen ring i lommen og hadde sørget for at den satt godt fast, så den ikke skulle falle ut. Hun skulle bli min.
Så gikk jeg videre, og hun sa: "Billy." Før jeg satte ringen på fingeren hennes, sa hun: "Synes du ikke det ville være mer gentleman-aktig å spørre pappa og mamma først?"
Jeg tenkte: "Å nei, nå skjer det igjen." Jeg svarte: "Ja." Så sa jeg: "Hør her, Hope, jeg vil fortelle deg noe." Jeg sa: "Når vi gifter oss, skal det alltid være femti-femti, ikke sant?"
Hun sa: "Det stemmer. Jeg skal holde min del."
Jeg sa: "Jeg skal holde min." Så sa jeg: "La oss begynne med det nå. Ser du?"
Hun spurte: "Hva mener du?"
Jeg sa: "Du spør moren din, og jeg spør faren din." Jeg visste at jeg kunne klare meg med faren hennes, men jeg var usikker på moren.
Hun sa: "Greit, det er i orden."
Jeg sa: "Se her, kanskje jeg bør spørre faren din først." Jeg visste at hvis faren hennes sa ja, hadde jeg i det minste et løfte å holde meg til.
Jeg husker hun sa: "Du bør spørre ham i kveld."
Jeg tenkte: "Å, litt raskt, men jeg antar jeg bør gjøre det."

text:
So that night we walked in, and he was sitting at his desk, typing out something. And I sat there. And she kept nodding her head to me, you know. See, it's nine o'clock, time … I had to go home at nine o'clock. And I thought, "It's late." So I got up and I started to the door, and she looked at me kind of strange, why didn't I ask her daddy?
[Brother Branham sighs.] I done like that, and she knowed what I meant. And her mother sitting back there, doing writing, or doing something. I thought, "Oh, my, I can't ask him right here, I would be asking them both, man. They would have it out right here, and them I would be left blank."
text_no:
Den kvelden gikk vi inn, og han satt ved skrivebordet og skrev noe. Jeg satt der, og hun nikket til meg gjentatte ganger. Klokken var ni, og jeg måtte dra hjem. Jeg tenkte: "Det er sent." Så jeg reiste meg og begynte å gå mot døren. Hun så på meg litt merkelig, som om hun lurte på hvorfor jeg ikke spurte hennes far.
[Bror Branham sukker.] Jeg hadde gjort en bevegelse som hun forsto betydningen av. Hennes mor satt lenger bak og skrev, eller gjorde noe annet. Jeg tenkte: "Å nei, jeg kan ikke spørre ham her. Jeg måtte spørre begge to samtidig, og det ville ført til en konfrontasjon rett der, noe som ville etterlatt meg i en vanskelig situasjon."

text:
So I walked to the door, and she walked over to the door with me. And I said, "I will come up Wednesday for church?"
And she said, "Uh-huh," and she just kept squeezing my hand.
And she pointed to her dad. I said, "Oh, I couldn't do that." I waited a little bit, I said, "Well, I got to."
I said, "Ahem. Mr.--Mr. Brumbach?"
He was typing along, you know, he said, "Yes!"
I said, "Could I speak to you out here just a minute?"
He said, "Yes, Bill, why? What do you what?"
I said, "Could I talk to you out here just a minute, Mr. Brumbach?"
text_no:
Jeg gikk til døren, og hun fulgte etter. Jeg spurte, "Kan jeg komme opp på onsdag til menighetens møte?"
Hun svarte, "Uh-huh," og klemte fortsatt hånden min.
Hun pekte på faren sin, og jeg sa, "Åh, det kan jeg ikke gjøre." Jeg ventet litt og sa så, "Vel, jeg må."
Jeg renset halsen og sa, "Hr. Brumbach?"
Han skrev, men svarte, "Ja!"
Jeg sa, "Kan jeg snakke med deg utenfor et øyeblikk?"
Han sa, "Ja, Bill. Hvorfor? Hva vil du?"
Jeg gjentok, "Kan jeg snakke med deg utenfor et øyeblikk, Hr. Brumbach?"

text:
And he said, "Sure." And he looked over to his wife, and his wife looked over at him.
I thought, "Oh!"
So I seen Hope walk over towards her mother, so I walked out on the porch; I got out there. And I done got too much nervous shock then, you know. So, I said…
He said, "What do you want, Bill?"
And I said, "Sure is warm tonight."
And he said, "Sure is."
And I said, "But, Charlie, it's a pretty night, ain't it?"
Said, "Yes it is."
I said, "You know, um, uh," I said, "I was a…"
text_no:
Han sa: "Selvfølgelig." Så så han bort på kona si, og kona så på ham. Jeg tenkte: "Åh!" 
Jeg så Hope gå mot moren sin, så jeg gikk ut på verandaen. Jeg hadde fått for mye nervøs sjokk nå, vet du. Så jeg sa:
Han spurte: "Hva vil du, Bill?"
Jeg svarte: "Det er virkelig varmt i kveld."
Han sa: "Det er det, ja."
Så sa jeg: "Men, Charlie, det er en vakker natt, er det ikke?"
Han svarte: "Ja, det er det."
Jeg sa: "Du vet, eh, um," Jeg sa, "Jeg var en..."

text:
He said, "Yes, you can have her, Bill, you can." I think a lot of him yet today.
I said, "Do you mean that I can…"
He said, "Yes."
Oh, my. Took a hold of that big old fat hand of his, I said, "Charlie, look," I said, "you know I am pauper." I said, "Your girl can dress nice, and everything, and I have one suit of clothes." I said, "But all my life I have been a vagabond, I've been in search of someone that I thought was a queen, one that I thought was a lady." I said, "I found that in Hope." I said, "I can't make her a living like you will, certainly not, Charlie, you make five hundred dollars a month and I am making about fourteen dollars a week."
text_no:
Han sa, "Ja, Bill, du kan ha henne, du kan det." Jeg tenker mye på ham fortsatt i dag.
Jeg sa, "Mener du at jeg kan…"
Han svarte, "Ja."
Å, min. Jeg tok tak i den store, gamle, fyldige hånden hans og sa, "Charlie, se. Du vet at jeg er fattig. Din jente kan kle seg pent og alt, og jeg har bare én dress." Jeg fortsatte, "Hele livet har jeg vært en vagabond, på jakt etter en jeg trodde var en dronning, en jeg trodde var en lady. Jeg fant det i Hope. Jeg kan ikke forsørge henne slik du kan, Charlie. Du tjener fem hundre dollar i måneden, mens jeg tjener omtrent fjorten dollar i uken."

text:
I said, "I got nine down there in the family, some of them beginning to work now," I said, "which will give me a release. But Charlie, I thought that there was no need in me taking much more of her time. As soon as the other boys get jobs and things can help me with … take care of my mother, I will--I will do everything that I can. I will work, Charlie, as long as there is breath in my body, I will slave and do everything I can, because I really love her. And I will do all that I can to be good to her. I will live true to her, I will do everything I can."
I never forget, the man's gone on now, he put that big arm around me, pulled me up close to him; just about the size of Brother Baxter. He reminds me a lot of him. He pulled me up to me like this, he said, "Billy," he said, "I would rather you would have her on them grounds than somebody that would mistreat her, no matter how much money he had." Said, "You will be more happy." He said, "Happiness does not consist of how much the world's goods you own, but how contented you are with the portion that's allotted to you."
"Thank you, Charlie, thank you."
text_no:
Jeg sa: "Jeg har ni der nede i familien, noen av dem begynner å jobbe nå," sa jeg, "noe som vil gi meg litt avlastning. Men Charlie, jeg tenkte at det ikke var nødvendig at jeg tok mer av hennes tid. Så snart de andre guttene får jobber og kan hjelpe meg med å ta vare på moren min, vil jeg gjøre alt jeg kan. Jeg vil jobbe, Charlie, så lenge det er pust i meg, jeg vil anstrenge meg og gjøre alt jeg kan, fordi jeg virkelig elsker henne. Og jeg vil gjøre alt jeg kan for å være god mot henne. Jeg vil leve trofast mot henne, jeg vil gjøre alt jeg kan."
Jeg glemmer aldri, mannen er borte nå, han la den store armen rundt meg, trakk meg tett inntil seg; omtrent på størrelse med Bror Baxter. Han minner meg mye om ham. Han trakk meg opp til seg som dette, han sa: "Billy," sa han, "jeg vil heller at du har henne på disse premissene enn noen som ville mishandle henne, uansett hvor mye penger han hadde." Han sa: "Du vil være mer lykkelig." Han sa: "Lykke består ikke i hvor mye av verdens goder du eier, men hvor fornøyd du er med den delen som er tildelt deg."
"Takk, Charlie, takk."

text:
She had asked her mother. And I don't know what happened in there, but anyhow, we got married. So…
When we got married, it was--it was a marvelous little old … I remember we was married down here in Fort Wayne, Indiana; we went home. I didn't even have… You know what, we…
I rented a house for four dollars a month. You can imagine what kind of a house it was; four dollars a month. Someone give us an old folding bed. How many knows what an old folding bed is? My, I seen Brother Ryan put up his hand. He slept on it enough, he ought to know. So he give us an old folding bed, and mama give us a little old iron bed stead little later on. We… First we had two rooms.
And I went down to Sears and Roebuck and got me a breakfast set that didn't … was painted. I think it cost us about three or four dollars. And I painted it yellow with a great big green shamrock on each one. And she was laughing at me, I will never forget it, about being an Irishman, painting the shamrock on it, and so forth.
text_no:
Hun hadde spurt sin mor. Jeg vet ikke hva som skjedde der inne, men uansett, vi giftet oss. Så…
Da vi giftet oss, var det et storslått lite… Jeg husker vi ble gift her i Fort Wayne, Indiana, og vi dro hjem. Jeg hadde ikke engang… Vet du hva, vi…
Jeg leide et hus for fire dollar i måneden. Du kan forestille deg hva slags hus det var; fire dollar i måneden. Noen ga oss en gammel sammenleggbar seng. Hvor mange vet hva en sammenleggbar seng er? Min gode, jeg så Bror Ryan løfte hånden. Han sov på den nok til å vite det. Så han ga oss en gammel sammenleggbar seng, og mamma ga oss en liten gammeldags jernseng litt senere. Vi… Først hadde vi to rom.
Jeg gikk ned til Sears og Roebuck og kjøpte et frokostsett som ikke var malt. Jeg tror det kostet oss omtrent tre eller fire dollar. Jeg malte det gult med en stor grønn kløver på hver stol. Hun lo av meg, jeg vil aldri glemme det, fordi jeg som irsk malte kløveren på det, og så videre.

text:
And we didn't have very much of the worlds goods. I went over to Mr. Weber, a junk dealer, and I bought me a stove for seventy-five cents and it cost me a dollar and a quarter to put new grates in it. I fixed it up and we went to housekeeping. Well, we were happy. We didn't have very much of the world's goods, but we sure had one another and the love of God was in our heart, and that's all we cared about. And I tell you, that's what really means something now. Yes, sir.
I look around, I heard somebody say, "Isn't that a beautiful home?"
text_no:
Vi hadde ikke mye av verdens gods. Jeg gikk til Mr. Weber, en skraphandler, og kjøpte en ovn for syttifem cent. Det kostet meg en dollar og tjuefem cent å sette inn nye rister. Jeg reparerte den, og vi begynte å holde hus. Vi var lykkelige. Vi hadde ikke mye av verdens gods, men vi hadde hverandre, og Guds kjærlighet var i hjertet vårt, og det var alt som betydde noe for oss. Og jeg må si, det er det som virkelig betyr noe nå. Ja, sir.
Jeg ser meg rundt, og jeg hørte noen si: "Er ikke det et vakkert hjem?"

text:
I said, "I don't know." Home is the not the house, it is the order of the house that makes the home. That's what makes home. No matter if it is a shanty, whatever it is, if the order is right on the inside, and godly, it's more of a home than if you had a palace somewhere. I would rather live in a shanty and be happy, then to live in a palace and be unhappy. That's right.
So I remember then, very well, we went ahead. And after a while, God gave us one of the greatest little gifts about a year after we were married. My, poor little boy, which is standing in the back of the building now. He … little Billy Paul, he came into the world.
text_no:
Jeg sa, "Jeg vet ikke." Hjemmet er ikke huset, det er ordningen i huset som skaper hjemmet. Det er det som skaper et hjem. Uansett om det er en rønne, eller hva det måtte være, hvis det er en gudfryktig orden på innsiden, er det mer et hjem enn et palass. Jeg vil heller bo i en rønne og være lykkelig enn å bo i et palass og være ulykkelig. Det er sant.
Jeg husker godt at vi fortsatte som planlagt. Etter en stund ga Gud oss en av de største gavene omtrent ett år etter at vi giftet oss. Min kjære lille gutt, som nå står bakerst i bygningen. Lille Billy Paul kom til verden.

text:
And I remember how we would go on. I was cutting up with her, and I said, "Now look, you know what we are going to call this? I said, "I believe he will be a boy. If it is," I said, "Now, for German…" She was a German, and I was an Irishman. And I said, "We will call him [unclear words] for German, and Michael [unclear words] Michael."
She said, "Oh, Bill, my, that's sounds horrible." So I … we went ahead and we was going on like that. And when God brought us the little boy how happy we were together. Went on, and life went on.
After while John Ryan, back there come into my life. I met him. He asked me to come to Dowagiac one day where--where he lives over there in Dowagiac, Michigan. Said, "Go on a little vacation." We saved out money and everything. And I had about oh, maybe ten or twelve dollars saved up.
text_no:
Jeg husker hvordan vi fortsatte. Jeg tullet med henne og sa: "Vet du hva vi skal kalle ham? Jeg tror det blir en gutt. Hvis det blir det," sa jeg, "så skal vi kalle ham noe tysk…" Hun var tysk, og jeg var irsk. Så jeg sa, "Vi kaller ham [uklare ord] for tysk, og Michael [uklare ord] Michael."
Hun sa: "Å, Bill, det høres helt forferdelig ut." Så vi fortsatte å tulle slik. Da Gud ga oss den lille gutten, hvor lykkelige vi var sammen. Livet gikk videre.
En stund senere kom John Ryan inn i livet mitt. Jeg møtte ham, og han ba meg komme til Dowagiac en dag, hvor han bor der i Dowagiac, Michigan. Han sa: "Ta en liten ferie." Vi hadde spart opp penger og alt. Jeg hadde kanskje omtrent ti eller tolv dollar spart.

text:
I'm fixing to come to the end of the story now, just in a little bit. I'm know I'm holding you, it's just I got about ten, twelve more minutes to be out on time. But we come to Dowagiac. I've tried to hold myself up and hit the high spots now, now pray for me.
When I went to Dowagiac with Brother Ryan back there, I went to his home, a little humble home about like I live in. His wife, but she would sware by him. He had a fine boy. And so they made me very welcome.
And on my road back home, going home, I come through Mishawaka. And I looked out there and there was the groups of people swarmed out there, and cars, and Cadillacs, and Fords, and cops, trying to keep order around. I thought, "What's going on here?" And I hear this singing, you know, and going on. My, everybody screaming and hollering. I thought, "Well, is it a funeral, or what's going on?"
text_no:
Nå skal jeg straks avslutte historien. Jeg vet jeg holder dere litt, men jeg trenger bare ti-tolv minutter til for å avslutte i tide. Men vi ankom Dowagiac. Jeg har prøvd å holde meg oppe og å treffe hovedpunktene, så vær så snill å be for meg.
Da jeg dro til Dowagiac med Bror Ryan, besøkte jeg hjemmet hans, et beskjedent hjem omtrent som mitt eget. Hans kone, hun ville sverge ved ham. Han hadde en flott gutt. De tok imot meg med åpne armer.
På veien hjem passerte jeg gjennom Mishawaka. Jeg så ut og så grupper av mennesker samlet der, og biler, Cadillacs og Fords, samt politifolk som forsøkte å holde orden. Jeg tenkte: "Hva skjer her?" Jeg hørte sang og rop. Alle skrek og ropte. Jeg lurte på: "Er det en begravelse, eller hva foregår her?"

text:
It was in a church house. And I stopped and drove in. Come to find out, it was a convention where there was a group of the pentecostal people was holding a convention over there. And they had to hold it in the north because of the race conditions they couldn't hold it, and it was an international convention. And they was holding it in a big tabernacle at Mishawaka.
So I never seen the Pentecost before, so I thought, "Well, believe I will go and see what it looks like. So I walked in and there they was all clapping their hands [Brother Branham claps his hands five times] like that, and a screaming and a singing. I thought, "What manners! Never seen anything like that in my life. What are they all talking about?"
And here was a colored man up there, and he was singing, and he was singing, "I know it was the blood," and all the congregation saying, "I know it was the blood." And here he would run down through there and grab somebody up and hug them like that. White, colored and all, saying, "I know it was the blood for me. One day when I was lost, He died upon the cross. I know it was the blood for me, running up and down the aisle." And I thought, "I never seen anything like that in my life." And how … I said… And somebody would jump up and scream and speak in tongues, and I thought, "Say, what is this anyhow?"
And then a preacher got up there and he got to preaching about the baptism of the Holy Ghost. And it looked like… And his finger was about that long, and he pointed me out right back in the back. He was talking to me. And I thought, "Say, how did that guy know anything about me?" See? And oh, there was hundreds and oh, it was thous… two or three thousand, I guess in the … all together, in the meeting.
text_no:
Det skjedde i et menighetshus. Jeg stoppet bilen og gikk inn. Det viste seg å være et stevne holdt av en gruppe pinsevenner. De måtte holde det i nord på grunn av rasesituasjonen, og det var et internasjonalt stevne. Det ble holdt i et stort tabernakel i Mishawaka.
Jeg hadde aldri sett pinsevenner før, så jeg tenkte: "Vel, jeg skal gå inn og se hvordan det ser ut." Da jeg kom inn, så jeg dem klappe med hendene [Bror Branham klapper hendene fem ganger] slik som dette, skrike og synge. Jeg tenkte: "For noen skikker! Har aldri sett noe lignende i mitt liv. Hva er det de snakker om?"
Og her var det en farget mann som sto og sang: "I know it was the blood," og hele menigheten svarte: "I know it was the blood." Han løp nedover og løftet opp noen og klemte dem. Hvite og fargede, alle sammen sang: "I know it was the blood for me. One day when I was lost, He died upon the cross. I know it was the blood for me," mens de løp opp og ned midtgangen. Jeg tenkte: "Aldri sett noe lignende i mitt liv." Og noen hoppet opp og skrek og talte i tunger. Jeg tenkte: "Hva er dette egentlig?"
Så kom en forkynner opp og begynte å preke om Den Hellige Ånds dåp. Det så ut som... Han pekte på meg med en finger, og jeg følte at han snakket direkte til meg. Jeg tenkte: "Hvordan visste den mannen noe om meg?" Og det var hundrevis, kanskje to eller tre tusen mennesker, samlet på møtet.

text:
And some group from up here at Chicago, colored group, they come up; called Locust Grove, or Piney Wood, or something like that, a quartet that … I never heard such singing in my life. Why, I thought, "There's one thing you have to say about them people, there are not ashamed of their religion. That's one thing sure. Their not ashamed of it."
So I thought, "You know, I believe I will come back tonight. And I went out and counted my money. I had just enough to get enough gasoline to come back, and twenty cents left. Well, I knowed how much gasoline it would take, and I couldn't get a tourist court. So I thought, "I will sleep out there in a cornfield." So I went down and got me twenty cents worth of stale rolls. And I thought, "I can live on them for a couple days, but I want to find out what this is all about." So I went out and got my rolls and put them in the back of my car, and went over.
text_no:
En gruppe fra Chicago, en farget gruppe, kom opp; de kalte seg Locust Grove, eller Piney Wood, eller noe slikt, et kvartett som ... Jeg har aldri hørt slik sang i mitt liv. Jeg tenkte: "Det er én ting du må si om disse menneskene, de skammer seg ikke over sin religion. Det er helt sikkert. De skammer seg ikke."
Så jeg tenkte: "Jeg tror jeg vil komme tilbake i kveld." Jeg gikk ut og telte pengene mine. Jeg hadde akkurat nok til å få nok bensin til å komme tilbake, og tjue cent til overs. Vel, jeg visste hvor mye bensin som trengtes, og jeg kunne ikke få et turistovernattingssted. Så jeg tenkte: "Jeg får sove ute i en maisåker." Jeg gikk ned og kjøpte meg tjue cent verdt med gamle rundstykker. Jeg tenkte: "Jeg kan leve på dem i noen dager, men jeg vil finne ut hva dette handler om." Så jeg kjøpte rundstykkene og la dem i baksetet på bilen, og dro tilbake.

text:
That night, He said, "I want all ministers," the spokesman said, "I want all ministers to come at the platform." There was just about, I guess about a two or three hundred of them at the platform. They were all white, colored, and all sitting on the platform. He said, "Now we haven't got time for you to preach, we just want you to come right down the row, and say who you are, where you are from." When it come my place, I said, "Evangelist, William Branham, Jeffersonville, Indiana," sat down. Next, next, next, on like that.
Come to find out, I was the youngest man was there, twenty-three years old then. I was the youngest man at the--at the platform. I didn't know it at that time. The next morning…
Well then we went on that night. And I want to tell you what happened that night. I heard all them ministers preaching that day about, oh, the deity of Christ, and the great messages about His walk on life, and His sacrifice, and so forth, and all the different things.
text_no:
Den kvelden sa Han, "Jeg vil at alle predikanter," ordføreren sa, "Jeg vil at alle predikanter skal komme opp på plattformen." Det var omtrent to-tre hundre av dem på plattformen. De var hvite, fargede, og alle satt på plattformen. Han sa, "Nå har vi ikke tid til at dere skal preke, vi vil bare at dere skal komme frem, oppgi hvem dere er og hvor dere kommer fra." Da det ble min tur, sa jeg, "Evangelist, William Branham, Jeffersonville, Indiana," og satte meg ned. Deretter fortsatte de andre på samme måte.
Det viste seg at jeg var den yngste mannen der, bare 23 år gammel. Jeg visste det ikke på det tidspunktet. Neste morgen...
Vel, vi fortsatte den kvelden, og jeg vil fortelle deg hva som skjedde. Jeg hørte alle de predikantene preke den dagen om Kristi guddommelighet, de store budskapene om Hans livsvandring, Hans offer og så videre, og alle de forskjellige tingene.

text:
But that night they brought an old colored man out, just a little bit of rim of white hair around the back of his head here, great big, long, felt preachers coat on, one of the old-fashioned, long frock-tailed coats with the velvet collar. Poor old fellow walked out there like this. And I thought, "That poor old man. Isn't that a shame?" I said, "Poor old dad." I said, "I guess he has preached a long time." And he stood there.
And I never seen a microphone before. I was a country preacher. So they had a microphone hanging up. It was something new then, you know.
So this old fellow got before, there, and he said, "Dear children." Uh-oh. He said, "I's wanting to take my text tonight from back in Job." Said, "Where was you when I laid the foundation of the world? Declare unto me where these … they are passing to. So when the morning stars sing together and the sons of God shouted for joy."
text_no:
Den kvelden førte de frem en eldre farget mann. Han hadde en tynn kant av hvitt hår rundt bakhodet og en lang, gammeldags forkynnerfrakk med fløyelskrage. Den stakkars gamle mannen gikk sakte fremover. Jeg tenkte: "Stakkars gamle mann. Er det ikke trist?" Jeg sa: "Stakkars gamle far. Jeg antar han har forkynnet i mange år." Og der sto han.
Jeg hadde aldri sett en mikrofon før. Jeg var en landsens forkynner, så mikrofonen som hang der var noe nytt for meg.
Denne gamle mannen stilte seg foran mikrofonen og begynte: "Kjære barn." Oh-oh. Han sa: "Jeg vil ta min tekst i kveld fra Job," og sa: "Hvor var du da Jeg la jordens grunnvoller? Forklar Meg hvor de går. Da morgenstjernene sang sammen og Guds sønner jublet av glede."

text:
I thought, "That poor old fellow. His preaching days are about finished. He's old." See?
Instead of coming down on the earth with it, like this, brother, he went back yonder about ten million years before the foundation of the world was ever laid, he climbed up into the skies, and he preached about what went on in the skies, the sons of God shouting for joy. He come on down through the dispensations and brought him back on the horizontal rainbow, back here, back over in the Millennium.
And about that time he got all happy. And when he did, he went, "Whoopee! Jumped up in the air, kicked his heels together, said, "Glory to God," said, "Hallelujah, there's not enough room here for me to preach." And off the platform he walked like that, like a kid.
text_no:
Jeg tenkte, "Stakkars gamle mann. Hans dager som forkynner er nesten over. Han er gammel." 
I stedet for å holde prekenen jordnær, som dette, Bror, gikk han tilbake omkring ti millioner år før verdens grunnvoll ble lagt. Han steg opp i himmelen og forkynte om hva som skjedde der, om Guds sønner som ropte av glede. Han kom ned gjennom tidsaldrene og førte oss inn på den horisontale regnbuen og inn i Tusenårsriket.
Omtrent da ble han svært glad. Da han ble det, ropte han "Whoopee!", hoppet opp i luften, klasket hælene sammen og sa, "Ære være Gud," og "Halleluja, det er ikke nok plass her for meg å forkynne." Og slik gikk han av plattformen, som et barn.

text:
I said, "Brother, if it will make an old man act like that, what would it do for me? I want that. That's what I want. That's what my heart hungers for, if it will make an old man act like that." I… That's what I wanted. I said, "Oh, my, them people has got something."
That night I went out in the cornfield, I thought, "I better press my trousers." So I took the two seats of my old Ford, and put them together, laid my trousers back and forth like this, and put the seats down to press them, laid down in some grass over at the side of the field out here somewhere in Indiana, out here.
text_no:
Jeg sa: "Bror, hvis det får en gammel mann til å handle slik, hva kan det gjøre for meg? Jeg ønsker det. Det er det jeg vil ha. Det er det mitt hjerte lengter etter, hvis det får en gammel mann til å handle slik." Det var det jeg ønsket. Jeg sa: "Å, min, disse menneskene har noe spesielt."
Den kvelden gikk jeg ut i maisåkeren og tenkte: "Jeg bør presse buksene mine." Så jeg tok setene fra min gamle Ford, la buksene frem og tilbake som dette, og brukte setene til å presse dem. Jeg la meg ned i gresset ved utkanten av åkeren her et sted i Indiana.

text:
And I laid there under that little old cherry that night. And I prayed, "God, somehow or another, give me favor with them people. That's what I want. Baptist or no Baptist, that's what I want. That's what my hunger … heart feeling for. That's what it's reaching for. There's a people that I have wanted to see all my life."
Next morning I went down. Nobody knowed me, you know. So I put on my little old seer-sucker trousers, and put a t-shirt on. Nobody knowed I was a preacher, so I went down. And I sit down. And when I sit down, here come a colored brother up and sit down the side of me; and over here sit a lady. And I sit down there.
text_no:
Den natten lå jeg under det lille gamle kirsebærtreet og ba: "Gud, gi meg på en eller annen måte velvilje hos disse menneskene. Det er det jeg ønsker – om de så er baptister eller ikke – det er det mitt hjerte lengter etter. Jeg vil møte disse menneskene som jeg alltid har ønsket å se."
Neste morgen gikk jeg ned. Ingen kjente meg, vet du. Så jeg tok på meg mine gamle bukser og en t-skjorte. Ingen visste at jeg var en forkynner, så jeg satte meg ned. En mørkhudet bror kom og satte seg ved siden av meg, og på den andre siden satt en dame. Jeg satt der.

text:
And when I got up that morning they was playing the music and everything. And there was a brother, his daughter come out and played a trumpet. Whitherspoon, I believe was his name. And he… That girl played the most beautiful of Blue Galilee that I sat there and cried like a baby. And I was sitting there.
Then up to the platform come a minister by the name of [unclear words]. He said, "Last night at the platform, the youngest minister we have here was an evangelist by the name of William Branham," said, "from Jeffersonville, Indiana." Said, "We want him to speak this morning."
Oh, my! My congregation! And I thought, "And seer-sucker trousers and t-shirt." So I just hunkered down real low like this, you know. In a few minutes … He waiting a few minutes, he got to the microphone again he said, "If there is anybody here knows where William Branham from Jeffersonville, an evangelist, was on the platform last night, we want him, this morning, to bring the message this morning. Tell him to come to the platform."
text_no:
Den morgenen da jeg sto opp, spilte de musikk og alt. En bror, hvis datter kom ut og spilte trompet, het Whitherspoon, tror jeg. Den jenta spilte den vakreste versjonen av *Blue Galilee*, og jeg satt der og gråt som et barn. Jeg satt der.
Deretter kom en pastor ved navn [uklare ord] opp på plattformen. Han sa: "I går kveld på plattformen var den yngste forkynneren vi har her en evangelist ved navn William Branham," og sa, "fra Jeffersonville, Indiana. Vi ønsker at han skal tale i formiddag."
Å, kjære menighet! Jeg tenkte: "I seersucker-bukser og t-skjorte." Så jeg krøp sammen så lavt jeg kunne. Etter noen minutter … Han ventet et par minutter, kom tilbake til mikrofonen og sa: "Hvis noen her vet hvor William Branham, evangelist fra Jeffersonville, var på plattformen i går kveld, ønsker vi at han skal komme opp og tale i formiddag. Si til ham at han må komme til plattformen."

text:
I scooted down real low, you know, like way down low. I thought, "Seer-sucker trousers, you know, and t-shirt." So I got real low. And I didn't want to get up before them people anyhow. They had something that I didn't know nothing about, so I just sat real still.
Directly, that colored man looked around me, said, "Say, you know him?" Uh-oh. Something had to happen. And I didn't … I knowed … I didn't want to lie to the man. I said, "Look, fellow, listen, I want to tell you something." I said, "I am he, see."
He said, "I thought you was getting down there kind of low about something."
And I said, "Well, look," I said, "are you a minister?"
Said, "Yes, sir." I said…
He said, "Go on up there, fellow."
text_no:
Jeg satte meg veldig lavt, som helt ned på gulvet, i seersucker-bukser og t-skjorte. Jeg ville ikke reise meg foran de menneskene uansett. De hadde noe som jeg ikke visste noe om, så jeg satt helt stille.
Etter en stund så den fargede mannen på meg og sa: "Si meg, kjenner du ham?" Å nei, noe måtte skje. Jeg visste at jeg ikke ville lyve for mannen. Jeg sa: "Hør her, kamerat, jeg vil fortelle deg noe. Jeg er han, skjønner du."
Han sa: "Jeg tenkte du satte deg så lavt for noe."
Jeg sa: "Vel, er du en forkynner?"
Han svarte: "Ja, sir." Jeg sa...
Han sa: "Gå frem, kamerat."

text:
And I said, "No--no--no--no, look, look." I said, "I want to tell you something." I said, "I--I--I've got on these seer-sucker trousers and this t-shirt," I said, "I don't want to get up there."
Said, "Them people don't care what you dress like, man. Get on up there."
